blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ X
INTERSECTION FORMALISM ON PROPER ALGEBRAIC SCHEMESby O. Jussila

(with an appendix by A. Grothendieck)

pages.title: 1. Compatibility of the filtrations with the composition law\(K'(X)\times K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\).

blocks.html:
1. Compatibility of the filtrations with the composition law
\(K'(X)\times K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\).

1.1. Let us first consider a noetherian scheme \(X\) and the group \(K_\bullet(X)\) of classes of coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules. For every coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(F\) we will denote by \(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F)\) its class in \(K_\bullet(X)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1.1.

For every integer \(n\in\mathbb Z\) we denote by \(\operatorname{Filt}_n(X)\) the subgroup of \(K_\bullet(X)\) generated by the classes \(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F)\) of the coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules \(F\) such that

\[\dim(\operatorname{Supp}(F))\leq n.\]
We thus define an increasing filtration \((\operatorname{Filt}_n(X))_{n\in\mathbb Z}\) of \(K_\bullet(X)\) such that \(\operatorname{Filt}_n(X)=0\) for \(n<0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1.2.

Let \(X\) be a noetherian scheme, \(F\) a coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-module such that \(\dim(\operatorname{Supp}(F))\leq n\), and \(X(n)\) (\(n\in\mathbb Z\)) the subset \(\{x\in X\ ;\ \dim(\overline{\{x\}})=n\}\) of \(X\). For every \(x\in X\) we denote by \(Z(x)\) the closed integral subscheme associated to \(\overline{\{x\}}\). Then the length of the \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\)-module \(F_x\) is finite for every \(x\in X(n)\) and non-zero for only a finite number of the \(x\in X(n)\), and we have
\[(1.1.3)\qquad \operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F)=\sum_{x\in X(n)}\operatorname{long}(F_x)\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_{Z(x)})+w\]
where \(w\in\operatorname{Filt}_{n-1}(X)\).
We deduce from this by immediate induction on \(n\):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.1.4.

For every noetherian scheme \(X\) and for every integer \(n\), \(\operatorname{Filt}_n(X)\) is the subgroup of \(K_\bullet(X)\) generated by the classes \(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_Z)\), where \(Z\) runs over the set of closed integral subschemes of dimension \(\leq n\) of \(X\).

Proof of 1.1.2: Let \(X'=\operatorname{Supp}(F)\). Since \(X'\) is a noetherian space of dimension \(\leq n\), the elements of \(X'\cap X(n)\) are maximal in \(X'\), hence finite in number, and \(\operatorname{long}(F_x)\) is finite for every \(x\in X'\cap X(n)\). This proves the first assertion.

Let \(K=\operatorname{Coh}(X)\) be the category of coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules and let \(K'\) be the subset of \(\operatorname{Ob}(K)\) formed by the coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules concentrated on \(X'\) which satisfy condition (1.1.3). Obviously \(K'\) contains the sheaves \(\mathcal O_{Z(x)}\) with \(x\in X'\). It therefore suffices to show that \(K'\) is an "exact" subset of \(\operatorname{Ob}(K)\), for then the dévissage lemma (EGA III 3.1.2) gives \(F\in K'\).
Let then
\[(1.1.5)\qquad 0\longrightarrow G'\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow G''\longrightarrow0\]

be an exact sequence of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules having two of its members in \(K'\). Then the sheaves \(G'\), \(G\) and \(G''\) are all concentrated on \(X'\), so the lengths \(\ell'_x\), \(\ell_x\) and \(\ell''_x\) of the \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\)-modules \(G'_x\), \(G_x\) and \(G''_x\) are finite
for every \(x\in X(n)\), and we have
\[(1.1.6)\qquad \ell_x=\ell'_x+\ell''_x\qquad(x\in X(n)).\]
Then the assertion (1.1.3) for all the members of (1.1.5) follows immediately from the hypothesis and from the equation
\[(1.1.7)\qquad \operatorname{cl}_\bullet(G)=\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(G')+\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(G'').\]
We know that \(K_\bullet\) is a covariant functor on the category of locally noetherian schemes with respect to proper morphisms. The following proposition asserts that our filtration is functorial:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1.8.

Let \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) be a proper morphism of locally noetherian schemes and let

\[f_*:K_\bullet(X')\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]

be the morphism induced by \(f\). Then

\[(1.1.9)\qquad f_*(\operatorname{Filt}_n(X'))\subset\operatorname{Filt}_n(X)\]

for every integer \(n\).
Proof: If \(F\) is a coherent \(\mathcal O_{X'}\)-module, then we have by definition (IV 2.11):
\[f_*(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F))=\sum_{i\in\mathbb N}(-1)^i\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(R^if_*(F)).\]
Now, the \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules \(R^if_*(F)\) are concentrated on \(f(\operatorname{Supp}(F))\) and by (EGA IV 5.4.1 (iii)) we have
\[\dim(\operatorname{Supp}(F))\geq\dim(f(\operatorname{Supp}(F))),\]
whence the assertion.

1.2. Now let \(X\) be an arbitrary scheme and consider the group \(K'(X)\) of classes of locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules (hereafter understood: of finite type). It is an augmented pre-\(\lambda\)-algebra over the ring \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\) (V 3.9.1). The map \(f\longmapsto f.1\in K'(X)\) allows us to identify \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\) from now on with a subring of \(K'(X)\). If \(E\) is a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-module, we will denote by \(\operatorname{cl}'(E)\) its class in \(K'(X)\). We define the operators \(\lambda^k\) (\(k\in\mathbb N\)) by
\[(1.2.1)\qquad \lambda^k(\operatorname{cl}'(E))=\operatorname{cl}'(\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits_{\mathcal O_X}^k E)\]
and by
\[(1.2.2)\qquad \lambda^k(x+y)=\sum_{\substack{i+j=k\\i,j\in\mathbb N}}\lambda^i(x)\lambda^j(y).\]

(V 2.2). Recall that in the \(\lambda\)-filtration \((\operatorname{Filt}^i(X))_{i\in\mathbb Z}\) of \(K'(X)\) we have \(\operatorname{Filt}^i(X)=K'(X)\) for \(i\leq0\), and that for \(i\geq1\), \(\operatorname{Filt}^i(X)\) is the ideal generated by the products
\[(1.2.3)\qquad P=\gamma^{i_1}(x_1)\cdots\gamma^{i_k}(x_k)\]

with \(1\leq i_1,\ldots,i_k\ ;\ i_1+\cdots+i_k\geq i\) and \(x_1,\ldots,x_k\in I=\operatorname{Ker}(\varepsilon)\) (\(\varepsilon\) being the augmentation homomorphism, cf. Exposé V 3.10). It was shown (V 3.10) that \(\operatorname{Filt}^i(X)\) is even the sub-\(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\)-module (hence the abelian subgroup, if \(X\) is connected) generated by the products (1.2.3), and where one may moreover assume that the elements \(x_p\) (\(p=1,\ldots,k\)) are of the form
\[(1.2.4)\qquad x_p=\operatorname{cl}'(E_p)-n_p,\]
\(E_p\) being a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-module of rank \(n_p\in H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\).
1.3. Recall (IV 2.10) that the bifunctor
\[(E,F)\longmapsto E\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}F,\]
where \(E\) is a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-module and \(F\) a coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-module, induces a composition law
\[(1.3.1)\qquad K'(X)\times K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]
which makes \(K_\bullet(X)\) a unitary \(K'(X)\)-module. The key result of the present exposé is the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.3.2.

Let \(X\) be a noetherian scheme. For every \(i,j\in\mathbb Z\) the composition law (1.3.1) induces by restriction a composition law

\[(1.3.3)\qquad \operatorname{Filt}^i(X)\times\operatorname{Filt}_j(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Filt}_{j-i}(X).\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3.3.

If \(X\) is a noetherian scheme of dimension \(\leq j\), then the canonical homomorphism

\[\theta_X:K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]

defined by \(\theta_X(x)=x.\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_X)\) (\(x\in K'(X)\)) induces for every \(i\in\mathbb Z\) a homomorphism

\[\operatorname{Filt}^i(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Filt}_{j-i}(X).\]
Proof: Let us first show that (1.3.3), for \(i+j\leq k\in\mathbb Z\) and for every closed integral subscheme of dimension \(\leq j\), implies (1.3.2) for \(i+j\leq k\).
Thanks to (1.1.4) it suffices to show that for every \(x\in\operatorname{Filt}^i(X)\) and for every closed integral subscheme \(Z\) of dimension \(\leq j\) we have
\[x\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_Z)\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j-i}(X).\]

To avoid any confusion, let us denote by \(\varepsilon_Z\) the class of \(\mathcal O_Z\) in \(K_\bullet(Z)\). If \(\alpha:Z\longrightarrow X\) is the canonical immersion, then the projection formula (IV ) gives
\[x\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_Z)=x\,\alpha_*(1_Z)=\alpha_*(\alpha^*(x)\,1_Z).\]
We have \(\alpha^*(x)\in\operatorname{Filt}^i(Z)\), so by (1.1.8) and (1.3.3) for \(X=Z\), \(i+j\leq k\), we find
\[\alpha_*(\alpha^*(x)\,1_Z)\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j-i}(X),\]
whence the assertion.

Theorem 1.3.2 is trivial for \(i+j\leq0\). Suppose it proved by induction for \(i+j<k\) and for every noetherian scheme \(X\). By the preceding remark it remains for us to show that for every integral noetherian scheme \(X\) of dimension \(j\) and for every product (1.2.3) satisfying (1.2.4), we have
\[(1.3.4)\qquad P\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_X)\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j-i}(X).\]
If \(k>1\) in (1.2.3), we are done thanks to the induction hypothesis and the associativity of the law (1.3.1). We may therefore assume (\(X\) being connected) that
\[(1.3.5)\qquad x=P=\gamma^m(\operatorname{cl}'(E)-n),\]
where \(m\geq i\) and \(E\) is a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-module of rank \(n>0\).
Let us distinguish two cases:
I. Suppose that one can find invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules \(L_1,\ldots,L_n\) such that
\[\operatorname{cl}'(E)=\operatorname{cl}'(L_1)+\cdots+\operatorname{cl}'(L_n)\in K'(X).\]

It follows \(V\) that \(x\) is the \(m\)-th elementary symmetric function in the \(\operatorname{cl}'(L_r)-1\) (\(r=1,\ldots,n\)), hence is a homogeneous polynomial of degree \(m\) with integer coefficients in the variables \((\operatorname{cl}'(L_r)-1)\) (\(r=1,\ldots,n\)). Applying again the induction hypothesis and the associativity of the law (1.3.1), we are thus reduced to showing that, for every invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(L\), we have
\[(1.3.6)\qquad (\operatorname{cl}'(L)-1)\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_X)\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j-1}(X).\]

The scheme \(X\) being integral, \(L\) and \(\mathcal O_X\) are isomorphic to submodules of the constant \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(R_X\) of rational functions on \(X\). The sum \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(F=\mathcal O_X+L\) is coherent, and we find

\[(\operatorname{cl}'(L)-1)\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_X)=\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(L)-\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_X)\\ =(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F)-\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_X))-(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F)-\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(L))=\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F/\mathcal O_X)-\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F/L).\]

But the fibers at the generic point of the sheaves \(F/\mathcal O_X\) and \(F/L\) are zero, so their supports in \(X\) are nowhere dense (\(X\) being irreducible), hence of dimension \(\leq j-1\). The assertion (1.3.6) follows immediately.
II. It remains to prove the general case. We introduce the flag bundle
\[g:D\longrightarrow X\]
of \(E\) over \(X\), where one can find invertible \(\mathcal O_D\)-modules \(L_1,\ldots,L_n\) such that
\[(1.3.7)\qquad g^*(\operatorname{cl}'(E))=\operatorname{cl}'(L_1)+\cdots+\operatorname{cl}'(L_n)\in K'(D).\]
Let \(e\) be the generic point of \(X\), and \(k(e)\) the residue field of \(e\). Then one can find a rational point \(u\) of the generic fiber \(D_e\) of \(D\)

over \(k(e)\), for these points are in one-to-one correspondence with the flags of type \((1,\ldots,1)\) (\(n\) terms) of the vector space \(E_e\) of dimension \(n>0\) over \(k(e)\). On the other hand these rational points also correspond to the germs of sections of \(g\) in a neighborhood of \(e\), so that one can find such a section \(s:U\longrightarrow D\) over an open set \(U\) of \(X\). Let \(X'=\overline{s(U)}\) be the closure of \(s(U)\) equipped with the induced reduced structure, and let \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) be the restriction of \(g\) to \(X'\). The morphism \(f\) is projective, and since it is an isomorphism over \(s(U)\), it is birational.
Hence \(\dim(X')\leq\dim(X)\) (EGA IV 5.5.6.1). On the other hand \(f(X')=g(X')\) is closed and contains \(U\), so that \(f\) is surjective and \(\dim(X')\geq\dim(X)\) (EGA IV 5.4.1 (ii)).
The hypotheses of case I are therefore satisfied for \(X'\) and \(x'=f^*(x)\in\operatorname{Filt}^i(X')\), and we find
\[(1.3.7')\qquad f^*(x)\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_{X'})\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j-i}(X').\]
We conclude from this, taking into account the projection formula and (1.1.8):
\[(1.3.8)\qquad x f_*(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_{X'}))\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j-i}(X).\]
On the other hand the canonical homomorphism
\[\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow f_*(\mathcal O_{X'})\]
is an isomorphism over the dense open set \(U\), and the \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules \(R^if_*(\mathcal O_{X'})\) (\(i\geq1\)) are concentrated on the nowhere dense closed subset \(X-U\). It follows that
\[(1.3.9)\qquad f_*(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_{X'}))=\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_X)+w,\]
where \(w\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j-1}(X)\). By the induction hypothesis we have
\[x\cdot w\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j-i-1}(X),\]
whence, taking into account the formulas (1.3.8) and (1.3.9), the assertion
\[x\,\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_X)\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j-i}(X),\qquad \text{C.Q.F.D.}\]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.4.

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian scheme. It should be noted that we have made use of only the following properties of the "dimension function" \(D(x)=D_Y(x)=\dim(\overline{\{x\}})\) on proper \(X\)-schemes \(Y\) in the course of this section:

(i) For every proper \(X\)-scheme \(Y\) and for every \(x\in Y\), we have \(D_Y(x)\in\mathbb Z\).

(ii) If \(y\ne x\) is a specialization of \(x\), we have

\[D(y)<D(x).\]

(iii) For every \(X\)-morphism \(f:Y_1\longrightarrow Y_2\) of proper \(X\)-schemes and for every \(y\in Y_1\), we have

\[D_{Y_2}(f(y))\leq D_{Y_1}(y).\]

(iv) There is equality in (iii), if \(f\) is an immersion or if \(Y_1\) is irreducible, \(f\) birational and surjective, and \(y\) the generic point of \(Y_1\).

This means that all the results of this section, in particular (1.3.2) and (1.3.3), remain valid as they stand, if one replaces the "dimension" by a function \(D\) satisfying conditions (i) to (iv) above and if one defines the filtration of \(K_\bullet(X)\) according to such a function \(D\).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 1.5.

Let \(S\) be a universally catenary locally noetherian scheme (for example a subscheme of a Cohen-Macaulay scheme). Set

\[(1.5.1)\qquad D_S(x)=n-\operatorname{codim}(\overline{\{x\}},S)=n-\dim\mathcal O_{S,x}\qquad(x\in S),\]

and we also set for every proper \(S\)-scheme \(s_Y:Y\longrightarrow S\) and \(y\in Y\):

\[(1.5.2)\qquad D_Y(y)=D_S(s_Y(y))+\deg\!\cdot\!\operatorname{tr}_{k(s_Y(y))}k(y).\]

If \(f:Y_1\longrightarrow Y_2\) is a \(S\)-morphism of proper \(S\)-schemes, the additivity of transcendence degrees and (1.5.2) immediately give

\[(1.5.3)\qquad D_{Y_1}(y)=D_{Y_2}(f(y))+\deg\!\cdot\!\operatorname{tr}_{k(f(y))}k(y),\qquad(y\in Y_1).\]

Let us show that the functions \(D_Y\) thus defined satisfy conditions (i)–(iv) of 1.4. By (1.5.3), condition (ii) is the only non-trivial one. Thanks to (iv) we may obviously assume that \(Y=\overline{\{x\}}\). We then factor the structural morphism \(s_Y\) of \(Y\) through its image \(s_Y(Y)=Z\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow S\), say \(s_Y=g\circ f\):

\[Y\xrightarrow{f}Z\xrightarrow{g}S,\]

where \(Y\) and \(Z\) are irreducible, \(f\) proper and surjective and \(g\) a closed immersion. Set \(x'=s_Y(x)\), \(y'=s_Y(y)\). We then deduce from (1.5.2) and (1.5.1):

\[D_Y(x)-D_Y(y)=\dim\mathcal O_{S,y'}-\dim\mathcal O_{S,x'}+\deg\!\cdot\!\operatorname{tr}_{k(x')}k(x)-\deg\!\cdot\!\operatorname{tr}_{k(y')}k(y).\]

Since \(S\) is catenary, we have
\[\dim\mathcal O_{S,y'}-\dim\mathcal O_{S,x'}=\dim\mathcal O_{Z,y'}-\dim\mathcal O_{Z,x'}\]
(EGA OIV 14.3.2.1), whence
\[(1.5.4)\qquad D_Y(x)-D_Y(y)= [\dim\mathcal O_{Z,y'}-\deg\!\cdot\!\operatorname{tr}_{k(y')}k(y)]-[\dim\mathcal O_{Z,x'}-\deg\!\cdot\!\operatorname{tr}_{k(x')}k(x)].\]
Since \(Z\) is universally catenary, (EGA IV 5.6.5.1) is applicable, and gives
\[C_1=\dim\mathcal O_{Y,y}-0,\qquad C_2=\dim\mathcal O_{Y,x}-0,\]
where \(C_1\) and \(C_2\) are the two terms on the right in (1.5.4). Then we conclude immediately from (1.5.4) that
\[D_Y(x)-D_Y(y)=\dim\mathcal O_{Y,y}-\dim\mathcal O_{Y,x}>0,\qquad \text{C.Q.F.D.}\]

We thus see that (1.3.2) and (1.3.3) remain valid for a universally catenary noetherian scheme \(X\) of dimension \(n\), if one defines the filtration of \(K_\bullet(X)\) by means of (1.5.1) according to the codimension of the supports. It should be noted that this filtration coincides with that of (1.1.1) when, in addition, \(X\) is biequidimensional.

1.6. One may ask whether it is always possible to define on a noetherian scheme \(X\) of finite dimension a function \(D\) possessing property (i) of 1.4 and moreover the following property, stronger than (ii)
(ii bis) If \(x\in X\), and \(y\ne x\) is an immediate specialization of \(x\), then
\[D(x)=D(y)+1.\]

It is obviously necessary for this that \(X\) be catenary, and sufficient that \(X\) be biequidimensional (EGA OIV 14.3.3). However, there exist noetherian schemes of finite dimension, catenary and equidimensional, having no such functions \(D\). To see this one can modify the example (EGA IV 5.6.11) in the following way: take two copies \(Y_1\) and \(Y_2\) of the spectrum of the ring \(B\) of loc. cit. and denote by \(y_i\) and \(z_i\) (\(i=1,2\)) the closed points of \(Y_i\) (\(i=1,2\)) corresponding to the maximal ideals \(\mathfrak m\) resp. \(\mathfrak m'\) of \(B\) of height 2 resp. 1. Recall that the residue fields of \(\mathfrak m\) and \(\mathfrak m'\) are isomorphic. One can therefore construct by gluing \(Y_1\) and \(Y_2\) a scheme \(X\) by identifying \(y_1\) with \(z_2\) and \(y_2\) with \(z_1\). One can then identify \(Y_1\) and \(Y_2\) with the two irreducible components of \(X\), and then in \(X\), \(Y_1\cap Y_2=\{y_1,y_2\}\) is of dimension zero. One then sees that \(X\) is a noetherian, catenary and equidimensional scheme (of dimension 2). If (ii bis) held on \(X\), we would have
\[D(y_1)=D(z_1)-1=D(y_2)-1\]
and on the other hand
\[D(y_1)=D(z_2)=D(y_2)+1,\]
so we arrive at a contradiction.
2. Snapper polynomials
2.1. Let us first recall the definition and some elementary properties of polynomial maps:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1.1.

Let \(f\) be a map from an abelian group \(A\) to an abelian group \(B\). We say that \(f\) is a polynomial map if there exists, for every sequence \(x_1,\ldots,x_r\) (\(r\geq1\)) of elements of \(A\), a family \((y(x_1,\ldots,x_r;k_1,\ldots,k_r))_{k_1,\ldots,k_r\geq0}\) of elements of \(B\), almost all zero, satisfying the following condition:

For every sequence \(n_1,\ldots,n_r\) of integers, we have

\[f(n_1x_1+\cdots+n_rx_r)=\sum_{k_1,\ldots,k_r\geq0}\binom{n_1}{k_1}\cdots\binom{n_r}{k_r}\,y(x_1,\ldots,x_r;k_1,\ldots,k_r).\]

We say that \(f\) is of degree \(\leq m\) if, whatever \(r\in\mathbb Z\) and \(x_1,\ldots,x_r\in A\), we have

\[y(x_1,\ldots,x_r;k_1,\ldots,k_r)=0\]

for \(k_1+\cdots+k_r>m\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.2.

Every homomorphism of abelian groups is a polynomial map of degree \(\leq1\). If \(h=f\circ g\), where \(f\) is a polynomial map of degree \(p\) and \(g\) a polynomial map of degree \(q\), then \(h\) is a polynomial map of degree \(\leq pq\).

Trivial proof.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.3.

Let

\[0\longrightarrow G'\longrightarrow G\xrightarrow{j}G''\longrightarrow0\]

be an exact sequence of abelian groups such that \(G'\) is divisible by

a prime number \(p\) and \(G''\) is a torsion group. Then every polynomial map \(f:G\longrightarrow\mathbb Q\) which factors through \(\mathbb Z\) is constant.
Proof: It is a matter of showing that for every \(x\in G\), we have \(f(x)=f(0)\).

a) Suppose first that \(x\) is a torsion element, say \(mx=0\) (\(m\in\mathbb Z\), \(m\ne0\)). Then the polynomial \(P_x(n)=f(nx)-f(0)\) has an infinite number of roots, namely all the integers divisible by \(m\). Hence the polynomial \(P_x\) is identically zero and we have \(f(x)=f(0)\).
b) Suppose now that \(x\) is a free element of \(G\) divisible by all the powers \(p^k\) (\(k\in\mathbb Z\)). Consider polynomials \(P_k\) defined by
\[P_k(n)=f(nx/p^k)=\sum_{i\in\mathbb N}n^i y(x/p^k;i)\in\mathbb Z\quad(n\in\mathbb Z\ ;\ y(x/p^k;i)\in\mathbb Q).\]
For every \(n\in\mathbb Z\) we have the identity
\[P_k(n)=P_{k+1}(pn)\]
\[\sum_{i\in\mathbb N}n^i y(x/p^k;i)=\sum_{i\in\mathbb N}p^i n^i y(x/p^{k+1};i),\]
from which we draw, by comparing coefficients,
\[y(x/p^k;i)=p^i y(x/p^{k+1};i)\quad(i\in\mathbb N).\]
But every polynomial \(P:\mathbb Q\longrightarrow\mathbb Q\) is continuous in the topology induced by \(\mathbb R\). Hence for \(k\) large enough we have
\[|P_x(n/p^k)-P_x(0)|<1.\]

But by definition \(P_x(n/p^k)\) is an integer for every \(n,k\in\mathbb Z\). It follows that \(P_x(n/p^k)-P_x(0)=0\) for \(k\) large enough. Hence the polynomial \(P_x\) is constant and we find in particular \(P_x(1)=P_x(0)\) i.e. \(f(x)=f(0)\).

The two cases a) and b) above show that the restriction of \(f\) to \(G'\) is constant. We will now show that \(f\) is constant on each class of \(G\) modulo \(G'\). For every \(b\in G\) we denote by \(g_b:G'\longrightarrow\mathbb Q\) the map defined by
\[g_b(y)=f(b+y)\qquad(y\in G').\]

The map \(g_b\) is a polynomial map from \(G'\) into \(\mathbb Q\), which factors through \(\mathbb Z\), hence constant by what we have seen above, whence the assertion that \(f\) is constant on the classes of \(G\) modulo \(G'\). It follows that we can write \(f=h\circ j\), where \(h:G''\longrightarrow\mathbb Q\) is a polynomial map which factors through \(\mathbb Z\). But by hypothesis \(G''\) is a torsion group, so that \(h\) is constant. Therefore \(f\) is constant, Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.4.

Let \(A\) be a commutative ring with unit and \(I\) a nilpotent ideal of \(A\). Let \(P\) be the multiplicative subgroup of \(A^*\) formed by the invertible elements of \(A\) of the form \(x=1+y\) with \(y\in I\), and let \(i:P\longrightarrow A\) be the natural injection. Then the composite map

\[\alpha:P\xrightarrow{i}A\xrightarrow{j}A\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\]

(where \(j(x)=x\otimes1\)) is a polynomial map. If \(I^{m+1}=0\), then \(\alpha\) is of degree \(\leq m\).
Proof: Let \(x_1,\ldots,x_r\) be a sequence of elements of \(P\) with \(x_i=1+y_i\) (\(i=1,\ldots,r\)). Then the binomial formula gives for every \(n_1,\ldots,n_r\in\mathbb Z\):
\[(2.1.5)\qquad x_1^{n_1}\cdots x_r^{n_r}=\sum_{k_1,\ldots,k_r\geq0}\binom{n_1}{k_1}\cdots\binom{n_r}{k_r}y_1^{k_1}\cdots y_r^{k_r},\]

where the sum on the right is finite since the elements \(y_1,\ldots,y_r\) are nilpotent. But the coefficients \(\binom{n_1}{k_1}\cdots\binom{n_r}{k_r}\) are polynomials with rational coefficients in the variables \(n_1,\ldots,n_r\) of degree \(k_1+\cdots+k_r\), so that \(\alpha\) is indeed a polynomial map. If \(I^{m+1}=0\), then \(y_1^{k_1}\cdots y_r^{k_r}=0\) for \(k_1+\cdots+k_r>m\). Therefore \(\alpha\) is of degree \(\leq m\), Q.E.D.

2.2. Consider now a noetherian scheme \(X\) and an element \(y\) of \(\operatorname{Filt}_j(X)\) (1.1.1). By associating to every \(x\in K'(X)\) the element \(xy\) of \(K_\bullet(X)\) we obtain a homomorphism of \(K'(X)\)-modules
\[\theta_y:K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]
which factors, taking (1.3.2) into account, through the canonical epimorphism
\[j:K'(X)\longrightarrow K'(X)/\operatorname{Filt}^{j+1}(X)=A.\]

Let \(\beta_y:A\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\) be the homomorphism thus defined. If we denote by \(I\) the ideal \(\operatorname{Filt}^1(X)/\operatorname{Filt}^{j+1}(X)\) of \(A\), we have \(I^{j+1}=0\). Keeping the notations of Lemma 2.1.4 we find a homomorphism of abelian groups
\[\gamma:\operatorname{Pic}(X)\longrightarrow P\]
which associates to the class of every invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(L\) in \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) the element \(j(\operatorname{cl}'(L))\) of \(P\).
From Lemmas 2.1.2 and 2.1.4 we immediately deduce:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.1.

For every noetherian scheme \(X\) and for every \(y\in\operatorname{Filt}_j(X)\) (\(j\in\mathbb Z\)) the composite map

\[\operatorname{Pic}(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q,\]

defined by

\[\operatorname{cl}_P(L)\longmapsto(\operatorname{cl}'(L)y)\otimes1\]

is a polynomial map of degree \(\leq j\).
2.3. If \(X\) is a scheme proper over a field \(k\) and if
\[g:X\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(k)\]
is the structural morphism, then the homomorphism
\[g_*:K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(\operatorname{Spec}(k))\simeq\mathbb Z\]

associates to the class \(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F)\) of every coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(F\) the Euler-Poincaré characteristic \(\chi(F)\in\mathbb Z\) of \(F\) over \(X\). From 2.2.1 we conclude immediately:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3.1.

For every scheme \(X\) proper over \(k\), and for every \(y\in\operatorname{Filt}_j(X)\), the map

\[S_y:\operatorname{Pic}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\lhookrightarrow\mathbb Q\]

defined by

\[\operatorname{cl}_P(L)\longmapsto g_*(\operatorname{cl}'(L)y)=\chi(\operatorname{cl}'(L)y)\]

is a polynomial map of degree \(\leq j\).

When \(L_1,\ldots,L_n\) are invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules and \(F\) is a coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-module such that \(\dim(\operatorname{Supp}(F))=j\), then 2.3.1 implies in particular that the Euler-Poincaré characteristic
\[(2.3.2)\qquad \chi(L_1^{\otimes m_1}\otimes\cdots\otimes L_n^{\otimes m_n}\otimes F)\]

is a numerical polynomial in the variables \(m_1,\ldots,m_n\in\mathbb Z\), of degree \(\leq\dim\operatorname{supp}F\), sometimes called the Snapper polynomial for \(F\) and the sequence \(L_1,\ldots,L_n\).
2.4. Recall [2] that the Picard functor \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}\) of \(X\) over \(k\) is the sheaf of groups in the (fpqc) topology of \(k\) associated to the presheaf
\[T\longmapsto\operatorname{Pic}(X\times_kT)\qquad(T\in\operatorname{Ob}(\operatorname{Sch}/k)).\]
When \(k\) is algebraically closed, it is known that the canonical homomorphism
\[\operatorname{Pic}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}(\operatorname{Spec}(k))\]
is an isomorphism.

When \(X\) is proper over \(k\), it is known [4] that \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}_{X/k}\) is representable by a group scheme locally of finite type \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}\) over \(k\). The identity component \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}_{X/k}\) of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}\) is a connected commutative algebraic group over \(k\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.4.1.

Let \(X\) be a scheme proper over a field \(k\), \(L\) an invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-module (resp. \(x\) an element of \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\)). We say that \(L\) (resp. \(x\)) is \(\tau\)-equivalent to zero, if there exists an integer \(n\ne0\) such that \(L^{\otimes n}\) (resp. \(nx\)) is algebraically equivalent to zero. The elements of \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) algebraically equivalent (resp. \(\tau\)-equivalent) to zero form a subgroup \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}(X)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\tau}(X)\)) of \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4.3.

For every scheme \(X\) proper over an algebraically closed field \(k\) the group \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}(X)\) is divisible by every integer \(n\) prime to the characteristic of \(k\).

Proof: Since \(k\) is algebraically closed, we have \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}(X)\simeq\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}_{X/k}(k)\). Since \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}_{X/k}\) is connected and of finite type, the \(n\)-th power endomorphism is surjective when \(n\) is prime to the characteristic of \(k\) (SGA 3 XV 1.3 and VI 1.3.2). But \(k\) is algebraically closed, so the corresponding endomorphism on the group \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}_{X/k}(k)\) is surjective, Q.E.D.
From 2.4.3 we conclude that in the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}^{\tau}(X)\longrightarrow T\longrightarrow0,\]

\(\operatorname{Pic}^{\circ}(X)\) is divisible by a prime number \(p\) and \(T\) is a torsion group, so that we can apply 2.1.3 to the restriction of the polynomial map (2.3.1) to \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\tau}(X)\). We thus find:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4.4.

The polynomial map \(S_y\) (2.3.1) is constant on the classes modulo \(\operatorname{Pic}^{\tau}(X)\).
Proof: If \(K\) is an algebraically closed extension of \(k\), we find a commutative diagram

\[\begin{array}{ccccl} \operatorname{Pic}(X)&\xrightarrow{S_y}&K_\bullet(\operatorname{Spec}(k))&\searrow&\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow&&\mathbb Z\lhookrightarrow\mathbb Q\\ \operatorname{Pic}(X_K)&\xrightarrow{S_y}&K_\bullet(\operatorname{Spec}(K))&\nearrow& \end{array}\]

where \(S_y\) is the polynomial map corresponding to \(S_y\) over \(\operatorname{Pic}(X_K)\). We may therefore assume \(k\) algebraically closed. The assertion then follows immediately from 2.1.3 and 2.4.3.
Remark 2.4.5. In Exp. XIII we will prove this result without using the representability of the functor \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}\).

pages.title: 3. Projection formulas for the associated graded objects

blocks.html: 3. Projection formulas for the associated graded objects

3.1. Consider a noetherian scheme \(X\). We denote by \(\operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X)\) the graded object associated to the \(\lambda\)-filtration of \(K'(X)\) and by \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X)\) the graded object associated to the filtration 1.1.1 of \(K_\bullet(X)\). Thanks to 1.3.2, the composition law (1.3.1)
\[K'(X)\times K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]
induces, by passage to the associated graded objects, composition laws
\[(3.1.1)\qquad \operatorname{Gr}^{i}(X)\times\operatorname{Gr}_{j}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_{j-i}(X)\qquad(i,j\in\mathbb Z).\]

\(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X)\) thus becomes a module over the ring \(\operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X)\) (and even a graded module over the graded ring \(\operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X)\), provided we change the sign of the grading of \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X)\) by setting \(\operatorname{Gr}^{i}(X)=\operatorname{Gr}_{-i}(X)\) (\(i\in\mathbb Z\)). Consider now a proper morphism
\[f:X'\longrightarrow X\]
of noetherian schemes. Then \(f\) induces a homomorphism
\[f^*: \operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X')\]
of graded rings \(V\), and by 1.1.8 a homomorphism
\[f_*:\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X')\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X)\]
of graded abelian groups. Then the projection formula (IV 2.11) and (3.1.1) give, by passage to the associated graded objects, the graded projection formula
\[(3.1.2)\qquad x\cdot f_*(y)=f_*(f^*(x)\cdot y)\qquad(x\in\operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X),\ y\in\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X')).\]

3.2. Let \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) be a projective complete intersection morphism (Exp. VIII) of virtual dimension \(r\) of noetherian schemes, \(X\) having an ample invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-module. In Exp. VIII it was proved that \(f\) induces a homomorphism
\[(3.2.1)\qquad f_*:\operatorname{Filt}^{k}(X')_{\mathbb Q}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Filt}^{k-r}(X)_{\mathbb Q}\qquad(k\in\mathbb Z)\]
which moreover satisfies the projection formula (Exp. IV):
\[(3.2.2)\qquad f_*((f^*x)\cdot y)=x\cdot f_*(y)\qquad(x\in K'(X)_{\mathbb Q},\ y\in K'(X')_{\mathbb Q}).\]
By passage to the associated graded objects in (3.2.1) and (3.2.2) we obtain a homomorphism of degree \(-r\):
\[(3.2.3)\qquad f_*:\operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X')_{\mathbb Q}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X)_{\mathbb Q}\]
and thanks to (1.3.2) a graded projection formula
\[f_*(f^*(x)\cdot y)=x\cdot f_*(y)\qquad(x\in\operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X)_{\mathbb Q},\ y\in\operatorname{Gr}^{\bullet}(X')_{\mathbb Q}).\]
3.3. Consider finally a morphism \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) of finite Tor-dimension of noetherian schemes. Then it is known (Exp. IV) that \(f\) induces a homomorphism
\[f^*:K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X')\]
such that
\[f^*(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(F))=\sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{cl}_\bullet\bigl(\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathcal O_X}_i(F,\mathcal O_{X'})\bigr)\]
for every coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(F\).

The \(\mathcal O_{X'}\)-modules \(\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathcal O_X}_i(F,\mathcal O_{X'})\) are concentrated on \(f^{-1}(\operatorname{Supp}(F))\) and when the fibers of \(f\) are everywhere of dimension \(\leq d\), then it is known (EGA IV 5.6.7) that
\[\dim(f^{-1}(\operatorname{Supp}(F)))\leq\dim(\operatorname{Supp}(F))+d.\]
It follows that \(f\) induces for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\) a homomorphism
\[f^*: \operatorname{Filt}_k(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Filt}_{k+d}(X'),\]
whence a homomorphism of degree \(d\) of graded abelian groups
\[(3.3.1)\qquad f^*: \operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X').\]

3.4. Suppose finally that the morphisms (3.2.3) and (3.3.1) are both defined for a morphism \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) such that \(r=d\). This is the case for example if \(X\) is quasi-compact with an ample \(\mathcal O_X\)-module and if \(X'=\mathbf P_X(E)\), where \(E\) is a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-module of rank \(r+1\). Then the projection formula in the derived category gives, for every \(x\in\operatorname{Filt}^{i}(X')_{\mathbb Q}\) and \(y\in\operatorname{Filt}_j(X)_{\mathbb Q}\), the projection formula
\[f_*(x\cdot f^*(y))=f_*(x)\cdot y\in\operatorname{Filt}_{j+r-i}(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]
When \(X\) is noetherian, then 1.3.2 allows us to deduce from it the corresponding graded formula.
4. Intersection numbers
4.1. Consider a scheme \(X\) proper over a field \(k\). By composing the product (3.1.1)
\[\operatorname{Gr}^{i}(X)\times\operatorname{Gr}_{i}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_0(X)=\operatorname{Filt}_0(X)\qquad(i\in\mathbb Z)\]
with the Euler-Poincaré characteristic
\[\chi:\operatorname{Filt}_0(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z,\]
we obtain a pairing called the "intersection number":
\[(4.1.1)\qquad \operatorname{Gr}^{i}(X)\times\operatorname{Gr}_{i}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z,\]
which we will denote by
\[(x,y)\longmapsto \langle x,y\rangle\in\mathbb Z\qquad(i\in\mathbb Z,\ x\in\operatorname{Gr}^i(X),\ y\in\operatorname{Gr}_i(X)).\]

Example 4.2. Suppose that the scheme \(X\) is of dimension \(n\). Take an element \(y\in\operatorname{Gr}_n(X)\), for example \(y=\operatorname{cl}.(\mathcal O_Z)\), where \(Z\) is an integral subscheme of dimension \(n\) of \(X\). Take also a homogeneous polynomial of weight \(n\)
\[P\in\mathbb Z[T_1,\ldots,T_n],\]

where \(T_i\) is given the weight (i) \((i=1,\ldots,n)\), for example a monomial \(T_{i_1}T_{i_2}\cdots T_{i_k}\) with \(i_1+i_2+\cdots+i_k=n\). When we substitute in \(P\) for each variable \(T_i\) \((i=1,\ldots,n)\) the Chern class
\[c_i(x)=\operatorname{cl}^i(\gamma^i(x-\varepsilon(x)))\in\operatorname{Gr}^i(X)\qquad(x\in K^*(X)),\]
we obtain an element
\[P(c_1(x),\ldots,c_n(x))\in\operatorname{Gr}^n(X).\]
We call the Chern number of \(x\) relative to \(y\) the integer
\[P_y(x)=\langle P(c_1(x),\ldots,c_n(x)),y\rangle.\]
When \(y=\operatorname{cl}.(\mathcal O_X)\), we omit the mention of \(y\) in the preceding terminology.

4.3. Without any hypothesis on the dimension of \(X\), consider a sequence \(L_1,\ldots,L_d\) of invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules and a coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(F\) such that \(\dim(\operatorname{Supp}(F))\leq d\). Set \(L_i=\operatorname{cl}^1(L_i)\) \((i=1,\ldots,d)\) and \(F=\operatorname{cl}.(F)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3.1.

With the notations above, for every sequence \(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_d\) of positive integers with \(\sum_{i=1}^d\alpha_i=d\), the coefficient of

\[\binom{n_1}{\alpha_1}\cdots\binom{n_d}{\alpha_d}\]

in the Snapper polynomial \(\chi(L_1^{n_1}\cdots L_d^{n_d}F)\) is equal to

\[(4.3.2)\qquad \langle c_1(L_1)^{\alpha_1}\cdots c_1(L_d)^{\alpha_d},[F]\rangle,\]

where \([F]\) is the class of \(F\) in \(\operatorname{Gr}_d(X)\). Therefore in the ordinary expansion of this polynomial in the monomials in the \(n_i\) \((i=1,\ldots,d)\), the coefficient of the monomial \(n_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots n_d^{\alpha_d}\) is equal to

\[\frac1{\alpha_1!\cdots\alpha_d!}\langle c_1(L_1)^{\alpha_1}\cdots c_1(L_d)^{\alpha_d},[F]\rangle.\]
In particular we immediately obtain the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.3.

The coefficient of the monomial \(n_1n_2\cdots n_d\) in the Snapper polynomial \(\chi(L_1^{n_1}\cdots L_d^{n_d}F)\) is equal to

\[(4.3.4)\qquad \langle c_1(L_1)\cdots c_1(L_d),[F]\rangle=(c_1(L_1)\cdots c_1(L_d),[F]).\]

Proof: Set \(L_i=1+M_i\) for every \(i=1,\ldots,d\). Taking the definitions into account we find immediately that the integer (4.3.2) is equal to \(\chi(M_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots M_d^{\alpha_d}F)\). When we substitute in (2.1.4) \(A=K^*(X)/\operatorname{Filt}^{d+1}(X)\), \(I=\operatorname{Filt}^1(X)/\operatorname{Filt}^{d+1}(X)\), then (2.1.5) gives (taking into account that \(M_i\in\operatorname{Filt}^1(X)\) for every \(i=1,\ldots,d\)) the congruence

\[(4.3.5)\qquad L_1^{n_1}\cdots L_d^{n_d}\equiv\sum_{\substack{\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_d\geq0\\\alpha_1+\cdots+\alpha_d=d}}\binom{n_1}{\alpha_1}\cdots\binom{n_d}{\alpha_d}M_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots M_d^{\alpha_d}\pmod{\operatorname{Filt}^{d+1}(X)}.\]

Therefore the coefficient of \(\binom{n_1}{\alpha_1}\cdots\binom{n_d}{\alpha_d}\) in the polynomial \(\chi(L_1^{n_1}\cdots L_d^{n_d}F)\) is equal to \(\chi(M_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots M_d^{\alpha_d}F)\), Q.E.D.

4.4. The integer (4.3.4) is sometimes called the intersection number of the invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules \(L_1,\ldots,L_d\) with \(F\), and it is denoted by \((L_1,\ldots,L_d;F)\) (cf. [3]). As a consequence of the expression (4.3.4) of this number we find at once the following properties:

1) The integer (4.3.4) is a symmetric and \(d\)-linear function in the variables \(L_1,\ldots,L_d\in\operatorname{Pic}(X)\), zero if \(\dim(\operatorname{Supp}(F))<d\), and it is "additive" in \(F\), i.e. additive in \(\operatorname{cl}.(F)=F\).
2) Using 2.1.2, we conclude from it immediately (with the notations of 1.1.2):
\[(4.4.1)\qquad(L_1,\ldots,L_d;F)=\sum_{x\in\widehat X(d)}\operatorname{long}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}(F_x)(L_1,\ldots,L_d;\mathcal O_{\overline{\{x\}}}).\]
3) Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\) and \(G\) a coherent \(\mathcal O_Y\)-module such that \(F=f_*(G)\), where \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) is the canonical immersion.
Then we find
\[(f^*L_1,\ldots,f^*L_d;G)=(L_1,\ldots,L_d;F).\]
More generally, the projection formula indeed gives
\[(4.4.2)\qquad\chi(f^*(x)\cdot y)=\chi(f_*(f^*(x)\cdot y))=\chi(x\cdot f_*(y))\quad(x\in K'(X),\ y\in K.(Y))\]
for every \(k\)-morphism \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) of proper \(k\)-schemes.
4) Suppose that in the morphism \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) above, \(X\) and \(Y\) are irreducible of dimension \(\leq d\). If \(f\) is of degree \(n\), then we have
\[(4.4.3)\qquad(f^*L_1,\ldots,f^*L_d;\mathcal O_Y)=n(L_1,\ldots,L_d;\mathcal O_X).\]
Let us recall the definition of the degree \(n\) of \(f\) [3]: it is zero except in the case where \(\dim(X)=\dim(Y)=\dim(f(Y))\). In this latter case we set
\[(4.4.4)\qquad n=\frac{\operatorname{long}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}(\mathcal O_{Y,y})}{\operatorname{long}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}(\mathcal O_{X,x})},\]
where \(x\) is the generic point of \(X\) and \(y\) the generic point of \(Y\).
In the proof of (4.4.3) the case \(\dim(X)=\dim(Y)\) is the only nontrivial one. If \(\dim(f(Y))<d\), then we have
\[(4.4.5)\qquad R^if_*(\mathcal O_Y)=0\]

for \(i>0\) in a neighborhood of \(x\). But condition (4.4.5) is also satisfied in the case \(\dim(f(Y))=d\), for then the dimension of the generic fiber is zero (EGA III 4.2.2 and EGA IV 5.6.6). Then 1.1.2 gives the congruences

\[f_*(\operatorname{cl}.(\mathcal O_Y))=\operatorname{cl}.(f_*(\mathcal O_Y))\equiv\operatorname{long}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}(\mathcal O_{Y,y})\operatorname{cl}.(\mathcal O_{X_{\mathrm{red}}})\pmod{\operatorname{Filt}_{d-1}(X)}.\]
By virtue of (4.4.2) it follows that
\[(f^*L_1,\ldots,f^*L_d;\mathcal O_Y)=\operatorname{long}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}(\mathcal O_{Y,y})(L_1,\ldots,L_d;\mathcal O_{X_{\mathrm{red}}}).\]
On the other hand 2° gives
\[(L_1,\ldots,L_d;\mathcal O_X)=\operatorname{long}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}(\mathcal O_{X,x})(L_1,\ldots,L_d;\mathcal O_{X_{\mathrm{red}}}),\]
whence the assertion.
4.5. We call numerical equivalence the equivalence relation defined by the intersection product \(\langle\ ,\ \rangle\):

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.5.1.

We say that two elements \(x\) and \(y\) of \(\operatorname{Gr}^i(X)\) \((i\in\mathbb Z)\) (resp. of \(\operatorname{Gr}_i(X)\)) are numerically equivalent, if they define the same homomorphism \(\operatorname{Gr}_i(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Gr}^i(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\)). We say that two invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules \(L_1\) and \(L_2\) are numerically equivalent, if their first Chern classes \(x,y\in\operatorname{Gr}^1(X)\) are numerically equivalent.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5.2.

An invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(L\) is numerically equivalent to zero if and only if

\[(4.5.3)\qquad\chi(L^n\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}_{\mathcal O_X}}\mathcal O_Z)=\chi(\mathcal O_Z)\]

for every \(n\in\mathbb Z\) and for every closed integral curve \(Z\subset X\).
This follows from 1.1.2 and 4.3.1.

We denote by \(\operatorname{Pic}^N(X)\) the subgroup of \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) consisting of the classes of invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules numerically equivalent to zero. From 2.3.4 we deduce

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5.3.

\(\operatorname{Pic}^\tau(X)\subset\operatorname{Pic}^N(X)\).
We will prove in Exp XIII that we even have \(\operatorname{Pic}^N(X)=\operatorname{Pic}^\tau(X)\).
5. The isomorphism \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\simeq\operatorname{Gr}^1(X)\)

5.1. Let \(X\) be an arbitrary ringed topos. Then we can define the ring \(K^*(X)\) and the filtration \((\operatorname{Filt}^i(X))_{i\in\mathbb Z}\) exactly as for schemes. Similarly we define the group \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) as the group of isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules. We denote by
\[(5.1.1)\qquad i:\operatorname{Pic}(X)\longrightarrow K^*(X)\]

the map which associates to the class \(\operatorname{cl}_{\operatorname{Pic}}(L)\in\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) of an invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-module \(L\) the element \(\operatorname{cl}^*(L)\) of \(K^*(X)\). This map is evidently a homomorphism from \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) into the multiplicative group \(K^*(X)^*\) of invertible elements of \(K^*(X)\). In what follows we will write the group law of \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) multiplicatively.
The map \(i\) has a left inverse defined by the functor "det". When \(E\) is a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-module of rank \(n\), we have
\[\det(E)=\mathop{\bigwedge}\limits^n E.\]
If
\[(5.1.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow E''\longrightarrow0\]
is an exact sequence of locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules, there is a canonical isomorphism
\[\det(E)\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}(\det(E'))\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}(\det(E''))\]
coming from (6.2.9) and from the fact that (5.1.2) is locally split. We thus obtain a homomorphism of abelian groups
\[(5.1.3)\qquad \det:K^*(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}(X)\]
which is the desired left inverse of \(i\). In particular \(i\) is injective, and in what follows we will identify \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) with its image in \(K^*(X)^*\).
5.2. We define another map
\[(5.2.1)\qquad \delta:\operatorname{Pic}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Filt}^1(X)\]
by setting for every \(L\in\operatorname{Pic}(X)\)
\[\delta(L)=1-L^{-1}.\]
Remarks 5.2.2.: a) Suppose that \(X\) is the locally ringed topos of Zariski sheaves on a scheme \(S\). When \(D\) is a Cartier divisor \(\geq0\) on \(X\) and
\[L=\operatorname{cl}^*(\mathcal O_S(D)),\]
then we have
\[(5.2.3)\qquad \theta_S(\delta(L))=\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_D)\]
where \(\theta_S:K^*(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(S)\) is the canonical homomorphism of 1.2.3. This follows immediately from the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\mathcal O_S(-D)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_S\longrightarrow\mathcal O_D\longrightarrow0.\]
b) Under the hypotheses of 5.1 and 5.2 we have for every \(L\in\operatorname{Pic}(X)\)
\[(5.2.4)\qquad \delta(L)\equiv L-1\pmod{\operatorname{Filt}^2(X)},\]
which justifies the notation \(c_1(L)\) introduced below. Indeed, we have
\[(L-1)-(1-L^{-1})=(L-1)(1-L^{-1})\in\operatorname{Filt}^2(X).\]
5.3. The composition of \(\delta\) with the canonical epimorphism
\[\operatorname{Filt}^1(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^1(X)\]
gives the "first Chern class" on \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\):
\[(5.3.1)\qquad c_1:\operatorname{Pic}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^1(X).\]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.2.

The map \(c_1\) (5.3.1) is an injective homomorphism, and the homomorphism

\[\det:\operatorname{Filt}^1(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}(X)\]

induces, by passage to the quotient, a left inverse homomorphism of \(c_1\):

\[\alpha:\operatorname{Gr}^1(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}(X).\]

If every section \(n\) of the constant sheaf \(\mathbb Z_X\) is bounded (for example if \(X\) is "quasi-compact", or if the final object \(e\) of \(X\) is a finite sum of connected objects), then \(c_1\) is an isomorphism and \(\alpha\) its inverse.
Proof: \(c_1\) is a homomorphism, for we have for every \(L,L'\in\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) the equation
Let us admit for the moment that
\[\det(\operatorname{Filt}^2(X))=\{1\}\in\operatorname{Pic}(X)\]
i.e. that \(\alpha\) is well defined. We then have
\[\alpha\circ c_1=\operatorname{id}_{\operatorname{Pic}(X)},\]
for by 5.2.2 b) we have:
\[\det(1-L^{-1})=(\det(L-1))=L\]
for every \(L\in\operatorname{Pic}(X)\). Next we show that
\[c_1\circ\alpha=\operatorname{id}_{\operatorname{Gr}^1(X)}\]
when every section \(n\) of \(\mathbb Z_X\) is bounded. Since \(\operatorname{Filt}^1(X)\) is the abelian group generated by the elements \(\operatorname{cl}^*(E)-n\), where \(E\) is a locally free
\(\mathcal O_X\)-module of rank \(n\), it suffices to show, taking 5.2.2 b) into account, that
\[(5.3.3)\qquad \det(\operatorname{cl}^*(E))-\operatorname{cl}^*(E)+n-1\in\operatorname{Filt}^2(X).\]

Since \(n\) is bounded, the final object \(e\) of \(X\) decomposes into a finite disjoint direct sum \(\coprod_i e_i\) of subobjects in such a way that \(n\) is constant on each subtopos \(X/e_i\) (\(i=1,\ldots,p\)). Since the functor \(Y\longmapsto K^*(X/Y)\) (\(Y\in\operatorname{Ob}(X)\)) transforms finite direct sums into finite direct products, we may assume that \(n\) is constant on \(X\), say \(n=n\in\mathbb Z\). Setting \(E=\operatorname{cl}^*(E)\), we find (cf. Exp. V):
\[\det(E)=\lambda^n(E)=\gamma^n((E-n)+1)=\sum_{0\leq i\leq n}\gamma^i(E-n),\]
which proves (5.3.3), the left-hand side being equal to \(\sum_{2\leq i\leq n}\gamma^i(E-n)\).
It remains for us to prove the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.3.4.

For every ringed topos \(X\) and for every \(x\in\operatorname{Filt}^2(X)\), we have

\[\det(x)=1.\]
Proof: Let \(\varepsilon:K^*(X)\longrightarrow\Gamma(\mathbb Z)\) be the augmentation homomorphism. Then (6.2.8) and (6.5.8) below give by linearity the formula
\[(5.3.5)\qquad \det(xy)=(\det(x))^{\varepsilon(y)}(\det(y))^{\varepsilon(x)}\qquad(x,y\in K^*(X)).\]
Hence it suffices to show that we have for every \(k\geq1\)
\[\det(\gamma^k(E-n))=1,\]
where, as above, \(E=\operatorname{cl}^*(E)\), \(n=\varepsilon(E)\). Now, we have
\[\gamma^k(x)=\lambda^k(x+k-1)\]
for every \(x\in K^*(X)\), so that
\[\det(\gamma^k(E-n))=\det(\lambda^k(E-n+k-1))=\det\left(\sum_{p+q=k}\binom{-n+k-1}{p}\lambda^q(E)\right).\]
\[\det(\lambda^q(E))=(\det(E))^{\binom{n-1}{q-1}}\]
for every \(q>0\), whence
\[\det(\gamma^k(E-n))=(\det(E))^r,\]
with
\[r=\sum_{\substack{p+q=k\\q\geq1}}\binom{-n+k-1}{p}\binom{n-1}{q-1}.\]
Now, \(r\) is a section of \(\mathbb Z\), everywhere equal to the coefficient of \(t^{k-1}\) in \((1+t)^{k-2}\), hence zero. We are done.

pages.title: 6. Appendix: Calculation of the determinants of locally free sheaves

blocks.html: 6. Appendix: Calculation of the determinants of locally free sheaves

6.1. Let us first consider an arbitrary ringed topos \(X=(C,\mathcal O)\). For every positive section \(n\in\Gamma(\mathbb Z_X)\) of the constant sheaf \(\mathbb Z\) and for every \(\mathcal O\)-module \(M\in\operatorname{Mod}(X)\), it is clear how to define the \(\mathcal O\)-modules
\[(6.1.1)\qquad M^n,\quad \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{n}}M,\quad \mathbin{\mathop{\wedge}^{n}}M,\quad\text{etc.}\]
The functors \((\ )^n\), \(\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{n}}\) and \(\mathbin{\mathop{\wedge}^{n}}\) transform free (resp. locally free) modules into free (resp. locally free) modules.
Generally, consider sections \(n_1,\ldots,n_r\) of \(\mathbb Z_X\) and a mult-functor (covariant, to fix ideas)
\[T:\operatorname{Mod}^{n_1}(\mathcal O)\times\cdots\times\operatorname{Mod}^{n_r}(\mathcal O)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(\mathcal O)\]
in \(r\) variables, where \(\operatorname{Mod}^{n}(\mathcal O)\) denotes the fibered category over \(X\) of locally free \(\mathcal O\)-modules of rank \(n\). Let
\[M=T(\mathcal O^{n_1},\ldots,\mathcal O^{n_r})\]
and
\[G=Gl(n_1)\times\cdots\times Gl(n_r),\quad\text{où }Gl(n_i)=\operatorname{Aut}_{\mathcal O}(\mathcal O^{n_i}).\]
It follows immediately that \(T\) defines a representation
\[(6.1.2)\qquad \alpha_T:G\longrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}_{\mathcal O}(M).\]
It is clear that if \(M\) is itself locally free of rank \(m\), for every composite functor,
\[T'T,\quad\text{où }T':\operatorname{Mod}^{m}(\mathcal O)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(\mathcal O),\]
we have
\[(6.1.3)\qquad \alpha_{T'T}=\alpha_{T'}\circ\alpha_T.\]
Conversely, given the representation \(\alpha=\alpha_T:G\longrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}_{\mathcal O}(M)\), we recover \(T(E_1,\ldots,E_r)\) (for every sequence

\(E_1,\ldots,E_r\) of locally free \(\mathcal O\)-modules of rank \(n_1,\ldots,n_r\)) by gluing the free \(\mathcal O\)-module \(T(\mathcal O^{n_1},\ldots,\mathcal O^{n_r})\) over trivializing local charts by means of the representation \(\alpha\). More precisely, to the sequence \(E_1,\ldots,E_r\) corresponds a right \(G\)-torsor \(P=P_{E_1,\ldots,E_r}\) such that we have a canonical isomorphism
\[(6.1.3)\qquad E_i\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}P\mathbin{\mathop{\wedge}^{G}}\mathcal O^{n_i}\quad(i=1,\ldots,r),\]
where \(\mathbin{\mathop{\wedge}^{G}}\) denotes the contracted product, and \(G\) acts on \(\mathcal O^{n_i}\) via the \(i\)-th projection \(G\longrightarrow Gl(n_i)\); then every representation
\[\alpha:G\longrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}_{\mathcal O}(M)\]
makes \(G\) act on \(\mathcal O^m\) by \(\mathcal O\)-automorphisms, and thus defines a mult-functor \(T=T_\alpha\) such that
\[(6.1.4)\qquad T_\alpha(E_1,\ldots,E_r)=P_{E_1,\ldots,E_r}\mathbin{\mathop{\wedge}^{G}}M.\]
Moreover, for every composite representation \(\alpha=\alpha_1\circ\alpha_2\) we find
\[(6.1.5)\qquad T_\alpha=T_{\alpha_1}\circ T_{\alpha_2}.\]

6.2. For every locally free \(\mathcal O\)-module \(E\) of rank \(n\) we will denote by \(\det(E)\) the invertible \(\mathcal O\)-module \(\mathbin{\mathop{\wedge}^{n}}E\). Keeping the notations of 6.1, the functor \(\det(T(E_1,\ldots,E_r))\) is determined, up to canonical isomorphism, by the corresponding representation
\[(6.2.1)\qquad \det\circ\alpha_T:G\longrightarrow\mathbb G_m=Gl(1).\]

Suppose now that \(X\) is the ringed topos of sheaves on the ringed site \((\operatorname{Sch}/S)\) equipped with the étale topology. Then we can calculate \(\det(T(E_1,\ldots,E_r))\), or what amounts to the same thing, the representation \(\det\circ\alpha_T\), by means of the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.2.2.

For every positive section \(n\) of \(\mathbb Z\), the étale sheaf of groups \(\operatorname{Der}(Gl(n))\) of commutators of \(Gl(n)\) is representable by \(Sl(n)=\operatorname{Ker}(\det:Gl(n)\longrightarrow\mathbb G_m)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.2.3.

Every representation

\[(6.2.4)\qquad \alpha:G=Gl(n_1)\times\cdots\times Gl(n_r)\longrightarrow\mathbb G_m\]

is of the form

\[(6.2.5)\qquad \alpha(Y)(s)=(\det(s_1))^{m_1}\cdots(\det(s_r))^{m_r},\]

where \(Y\in\operatorname{Ob}(\operatorname{Sch}/S)\), \(s=(s_1,\ldots,s_r)\in G(Y)\) and \(m_1,\ldots,m_r\) are sections of \(\mathbb Z\) uniquely determined by \(\alpha\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.2.6.

With the notations of 6.1, we have

\[\det(T_\alpha(E_1,\ldots,E_r))=\det(E_1)^{\otimes m_1}\otimes\cdots\otimes\det(E_r)^{\otimes m_r},\]

where \(m_1,\ldots,m_r\) are sections of \(\mathbb Z\) uniquely determined by \(\alpha\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.2.7.

We can determine the exponents \(m_1,\ldots,m_r\) in (6.2.5) and (6.2.6) by successively substituting in (6.2.5)

\[s_i=\lambda\cdot\operatorname{id}\ (\lambda\in\mathbb G_m(Y)),\qquad s_j=\operatorname{id}_{Gl(n_j)}(Y)\quad\text{pour }j\ne i\ (i,j=1,\ldots,r),\]

whence

\[\det(s_i)=\lambda^{n_i}\quad\text{et}\quad\alpha(Y)(s)=\lambda^{n_i m_i}.\]

For example one thus deduces from (6.2.6)

\[(6.2.8)\qquad \det(E_1\otimes E_2)=(\det(E_1))^{\otimes n_2}\otimes(\det(E_2))^{\otimes n_1},\]
\[(6.2.9)\qquad \det(E_1\oplus E_2)=(\det(E_1))\otimes(\det(E_2))\]

and

\[(6.2.10)\qquad \det(\mathbin{\mathop{\wedge}^{m}}E_1)=(\det(E_1))^{\otimes\binom{n_1-1}{m-1}},\]

etc.
6.3. Let us first show how 6.2.3 follows from 6.2.2. For this it evidently suffices to prove (6.2.5) for the
\[s_{(i)}=(1,\ldots,s_i,\ldots,1)\quad(i=1,\ldots,r),\]
hence we may assume \(r=1\), \(G=Gl(n_1)\). By 6.2.2, \(\alpha\) factors through \(Gl(n_1)/Sl(n_1)\), i.e. there exists a commutative diagram
\[\begin{array}{ccc}Gl(n_1)&\xrightarrow{\alpha}&\mathbb G_m\\ {\scriptstyle j}\downarrow&&\nearrow{\scriptstyle\beta}\\ Gl(n_1)/Sl(n_1)&&\end{array}\]
where \(j\) is the canonical epimorphism and \(\beta\) a representation of \(Gl(n_1)/Sl(n_1)\) in \(\mathbb G_m\). But the homomorphism
\[\det:Gl(n_1)\longrightarrow\mathbb G_m\]
has a section, so that the monomorphism
\[Gl(n_1)/Sl(n_1)\longrightarrow\mathbb G_m\]
induced by \(\det\) is an isomorphism. On the other hand it is known (SGA 3 VIII 1.3) that every representation of \(\mathbb G_m\) in itself is of the form
\[s\longmapsto s^m,\]
where \(m\) is a section of \(\mathbb Z\), whence the assertion.
Corollary 6.2.6 is a simple reformulation of 6.2.3. Note that 6.2.3 also follows immediately from SGA 3 XXII 6.2.1, but we wanted to give it here an elementary proof via 6.2.2).

6.4. Proof of 6.2.2. It suffices to show that, whatever \(Y\in\operatorname{Ob}(\operatorname{Sch}/S)\), \(y\in Y\) and \(s\in Sl(n)(Y)\) may be, we can find an "étale neighborhood" \(Y'\longrightarrow Y\) of \(y\) such that the restriction of \(s\) to \(Y'\) is a product of commutators of \(Gl(n)(Y')\). The question being local, we may assume \(Y\) affine with ring \(A\), and \(n\) constant equal to \(n\) on \(Y\). Let \(\mathfrak p\) be the prime ideal of \(y\) and \((s_{ij})_{(i,j=1,\ldots,n)}\) the matrix of \(s\). For every \(\lambda\in A\) and \(h,k=1,\ldots,n\) ; \(h\ne k\), we will denote by \(c_{h,k,\lambda}\) the matrix \((c_{ij})_{(i,j=1,\ldots,n)}\) such that \(c_{hk}=\lambda\) and \(c_{ij}=\delta_{ij}\) for \((i,j)\ne(h,k)\) (\(\delta_{ij}=\) the Kronecker symbol). For every matrix \(a=(a_{ij})_{(i,j=1,\ldots,n)}\) we obtain \(c_{h,k,\lambda}a\) by adding to the \(k\)-th column of \(a\) \(\lambda\) times the \(h\)-th. Similarly we obtain the matrix \(ac_{h,k,\lambda}\) by adding to the \(h\)-th row of \(a\) \(\lambda\) times the \(k\)-th. In particular we have
\[\det(c_{h,k,\lambda})=1,\]
and one sees at once that we have
\[(6.3.1)\qquad c_{h,k,\lambda}\,c_{h,k,\mu}=c_{h,k,\lambda+\mu}\]
for every \(\lambda,\mu\in A\), so that we find a representation
\[c_{h,k}:\mathbb G_a\longrightarrow Sl(n)\qquad(h,k=1,\ldots,n\ ;\ h\ne k).\]

Unless expressly stated otherwise, we will assume in what follows that the \(\lambda\) occurring in \(c_{h,k,\lambda}\) (\(\lambda\in A\) ; \(h,k=1,\ldots,n\) ; \(h\ne k\)) is invertible. The proof then proceeds in two steps. We will first show that \(s\) is a product of matrices \(c_{h,k,\lambda}\) in a sufficiently small Zariski neighborhood of \(y\). Then we will show that the matrices \(c_{h,k,\lambda}\) are products of commutators of \(Gl(n)\) in a sufficiently small étale neighborhood of \(y\).

For the first, it is a matter of transforming \(s\) into the identity matrix \(1=(\delta_{ij})\) in a Zariski neighborhood of \(y\) by multiplying \(s\) on the left and on the right by suitable matrices \(c_{h,k,\lambda}\). Let us first show that we can in this way arrange that \(s_{11}=1\). If \(s_{11} e1\) and \(s_{21}\in\mathfrak p\), we look for a matrix \(c=c_{h,1,\lambda}\) such that the product \(s'=sc=(s'_{ij})\) has \(s'_{21} otin\mathfrak p\). Since \(\det(s)=1 otin\mathfrak p\), there exists at least one index \(i\) such that \(s_{i1} otin\mathfrak p\). Then we have \(s_{21}+s_{i1} otin\mathfrak p\), and \(c_{i,1,1}\) answers the question. We may therefore assume \(s_{21} otin\mathfrak p\), and localizing if necessary, we may assume \(s_{21}\) invertible. If \(s_{11}-1\in\mathfrak p\), then in \(s'=(s'_{ij})=sc_{1,2,1}\) we have \(s'_{11}-1=(s_{11}+s_{21})-1 otin\mathfrak p\). Localizing if necessary, we may then assume \(s_{11}-1\) invertible. Then \(\lambda=(1-s_{11})/s_{21}\) is invertible and we find \(s'_{11}=1\) in \(s'=(s'_{ij})=sc_{1,2,\lambda}\).
Assuming \(s_{11}=1\) we can then bring \(s\) to the form where
\[s_{11}=1,\quad s_{1i}=s_{i1}=0\qquad(i=2,\ldots,n)\]

in a Zariski neighborhood of \(y\) by multiplying \(s\) on the left (resp. on the right) by matrices \(c_{i,1,\lambda}\) (resp. \(c_{1,i,\lambda}\)). Then we arrange that \(s_{22}=1\) etc. By induction we can then transform \(s\) in a Zariski neighborhood \(U\) of \(y\) into a matrix \(s'=(s'_{ij})\) having \(s'_{ij}=\delta_{ij}\) for \(i<n\) or \(j<n\). But by the very construction we then have \(s'=c_1sc_2\) on \(U\), where \(c_1\) and \(c_2\) are products of matrices \(c_{h,k,\lambda}\). Hence we have
\[\det(s')=\det(s)=1,\]
so that \(s'_{nn}=1\), \(s'=1\), and we are done.

We therefore assume that \(s\) is already on \(Y\) a product of matrices \(c_{h,k,\lambda}\). It remains to see that these are products of commutators in an étale neighborhood of \(y\). First of all one sees that any two matrices
\(c_{h,k,\lambda}\) and \(c_{h,k,\mu}\) whatsoever are conjugate, in other words there exists an invertible matrix \(a=(a_{ij})\) (\(i,j=1,\ldots,n\)) such that we have
\[ac_{h,k,\lambda}=c_{h,k,\mu}a.\]

Indeed, the matrix (\(a_{ij}\)) with \(a_{hh}=1/\mu\), \(a_{kk}=1/\lambda\), and moreover \(a_{ij}=\delta_{ij}\), answers the question (recall that \(\lambda\) and \(\mu\) are by hypothesis invertible and \(h\ne k\)). Hence for an arbitrary homomorphism \(\beta\) from \(Gl(n)(U)\) into a sheaf of abelian groups \(H\), we have
\[\beta(c_{h,k,\lambda})=\beta(c_{h,k,\mu}).\]

If \(\lambda+\mu\) is invertible, one deduces from this and from (6.3.1) that \(\beta(c_{h,k,\lambda})=0\) for every \(h,k,\lambda\), so that \(c_{h,k,\lambda}\) is a product of commutators. If the residue field \(k\) of \(y\) is not the prime field \(\mathbb F_2=[0,1]\), then for every invertible \(\lambda\in A\) we can find a Zariski neighborhood \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\) of \(y\), and an invertible element \(\mu\in B\) such that \(\lambda+\mu\in B\) is invertible. It follows that \(c_{h,k,\lambda}\) is a product of commutators on \(U\).
If \(k=\mathbb F_2\), then \(V=\operatorname{Spec}(A[T]/(T^2+T+1))\) is an étale neighborhood of \(y\), whose residue field is different from \(\mathbb F_2\). We are done.
6.5. Remarks

6.5.1. When the residue fields of \(S\) are all non-isomorphic to \(\mathbb F_2\), the preceding proof shows that 6.2.2 remains valid for the ringed topos of Zariski sheaves on \(S\). On the other hand, already for \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_2)\), \(Sl(2,\mathbb F_2)=Gl(2,\mathbb F_2)\) is different from the group of commutators of \(Gl(2,\mathbb F_2)\). The order of the latter is 3 while the order of \(Sl(2,\mathbb F_2)\) is 6 (cf. [1]).
6.5.2. On an arbitrary locally ringed topos \(X\) it is not true in general that every representation \(G_m\longrightarrow G_m\) is defined by a
power. For example on the topos \((Ens)\) ringed by the field (mathbb Q), the representation \(G_m\longrightarrow G_m\) defined by
\[r\longmapsto |r|\qquad(r\in\mathbb Q^*)\]
is not a power. In particular 6.2.3 does not remain valid for an arbitrary locally ringed topos \(X\) and for an arbitrary representation \(\alpha:G\longrightarrow G_m\).

6.5.3. On the other hand 6.2.3 remains valid for the representations associated with the formulas (6.2.8), (6.2.9) and (6.2.10) on an arbitrary ringed topos \(X=(C,\mathcal O)\), because these representations are "algebraic" in the following sense:
Let \(e\) be the final object of \(X\), \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O(e))\), and consider the functor
\[u:C\longrightarrow(\operatorname{Sch}/S)\]

which associates to each \(Y\in\operatorname{Ob}(C)\) the affine scheme \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O(Y))\) over \(S\). Then for every positive integer \(n\) the presheaf \(Gl(n)_X\) on \(C\) is the composite with \(u\) of the corresponding presheaf \(Gl(n)_S\) on (\(\operatorname{Sch}/S\)). We say that a representation on \(X\)
\[(6.5.4)\qquad \alpha:Gl(n)\longrightarrow G_m\]
is algebraic if it comes via \(u\) from a representation
\[(6.5.5)\qquad \alpha_S:Gl(n)_S\longrightarrow G_{m,S}\]
of presheaves of groups on (\(\operatorname{Sch}/S\)), i.e., if we have
\[\alpha(s)=\alpha_S(s)\]
for every \(Y\in\operatorname{Ob}(C)\), \(s\in Gl(n)(Y)=Gl(n)_S(u(Y))\).

The representation \(\alpha\) in (6.5.4) is of course completely determined by \(\alpha_S\). It follows immediately that (6.2.3) remains valid in an arbitrary ringed topos when \(\alpha\) is algebraic. Hence in particular the formulas (6.2.8), (6.2.9) and (6.2.10) remain valid on an arbitrary ringed topos.

pages.title: 7. Appendix: Specialization in intersection theory by A. Grothendieck

blocks.html: 7. Appendix: Specialization in intersection theoryby A. Grothendieck

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.1.

Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be a regular closed immersion (VII 1.4), of bounded codimension, so that \(i\) is a perfect morphism (VII 1.9), and we can therefore define the morphism (IV 2.12)

\[i^*:K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(Y).\]

Let on the other hand \(N\) be the conormal sheaf for \(i\), which is a locally free sheaf of finite type on \(Y\) (VII 1.6), so that we can form

\[\lambda_{-1}(N)\in K'(Y).\]

Consider also the direct image homomorphism (IV 2.11)

\[i_*:K_\bullet(Y)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X).\]

This being so, we have the formula

\[i^*i_*(x)=x\,\lambda_{-1}(N)\qquad\text{pour }x\in K_\bullet(Y),\]

where the product on the right-hand side is relative to the \(K'(Y)\)-module structure of \(K_\bullet(Y)\) defined in (IV 2.10).
The proof is, essentially without change, the same as for the same formula, written working with \(K'(X)\), \(K'(Y)\), established in (VII 2.7).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) be a flat morphism, with \(S\) a trait, i.e. the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring \(A\), \(s\) the closed point of \(S\), \(t\) its generic point, \(i:X_s\longrightarrow X\) the canonical inclusion. Then \(i\) is of finite tor-dimension, hence (IV 2.12) we can define

\[i^*:K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s),\]

on the other hand (IV 2.11) we have a homomorphism

\[i_*:K_\bullet(X_s)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X).\]

This being so, the composite

\[i^*i_*:K_\bullet(X_s)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s)\]

is zero.

Indeed, \(i:X_s\longrightarrow X\) is a regular immersion of codimension 1, hence the conormal sheaf \(N\) is free of rank 1, since \(X_s\) can be described by a global equation \(f^*(g)=0\) (where \(g\) is a uniformizer of \(A\)). We therefore have \(\lambda_{-1}(N)=0\), and we conclude thanks to 7.1.

We will assume in all that follows that \(X\) is noetherian and flat over \(S\). Let us now apply the homotopy exact sequence (IX 1.1) to the situation: \(X\), equipped with the open set \(X_t\) and the complementary closed subscheme \(X_s\):
\[K_\bullet(X_s)\xrightarrow{i_*}K_\bullet(X)\xrightarrow{j^*}K_\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow0,\]
where \(j:X_t\longrightarrow X\) is the inclusion, which is an open immersion. Using 7.2, we find:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.3.

With the notations of 7.2, \(X\) being assumed noetherian, there exists a unique group homomorphism

\[(7.3.1)\qquad \sigma:K_\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s)\]

(called the "specialization homomorphism") making the diagram commutative

\[(7.3.2)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} K_\bullet(X)&\xrightarrow{j^*}&K_\bullet(X_t)\\ {\scriptstyle i^*}\downarrow&\swarrow{\scriptstyle\sigma}&\\ K_\bullet(X_s)&& \end{array}\]

When we regard the two sides of (7.3.1) as modules over \(K'(X)\) by means of the ring homomorphisms \(K'(X)\xrightarrow{j^*}K'(X_t)\) and \(K'(X)\xrightarrow{i^*}K'(X_s)\) (IV 2.7 b) and the module structures of \(K_\bullet(X_t)\) and \(K_\bullet(X_s)\) over \(K'(X_t)\) and \(K'(X_s)\) respectively (IV 2.10), the homomorphism \(\sigma\) is a homomorphism of \(K'(X)\)-modules.

The last assertion comes from the fact that \(i^*:K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s)\) is a homomorphism of \(K'(X)\)-modules, as is the epimorphism \(j^*:K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_t)\), by virtue of (IV 2.12.2).
We shall now review other properties of the specialization morphism.

7.4. Compatibility with the filtrations. Let us equip the \(K_\bullet\) with the filtration \(\operatorname{Fil}_\bullet K_\bullet\) by dimension of supports (1.1.1). Then, if \(f\) is of finite type, more generally if \(X\) is catenary, the specialization homomorphism is compatible with these filtrations, and consequently induces a homomorphism (also called the specialization homomorphism):
\[(7.4.1)\qquad \sigma_\bullet:\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_s).\]

To see this, we are reduced by 1.14 to proving that if \(Y_t\) is an integral subscheme of dimension \(d\) of \(X_t\), then \(\sigma_\bullet(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(Y_t))\) is of filtration \(\leq d\). Now let \(Y\) be the schematic closure of \(Y_t\) in \(X\), so that \(Y\) is flat over \(S\) (EGA IV 2.8.1). It follows easily that \(i^*(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(Y))=\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(Y_s)\), where the two \(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet\) are taken respectively in \(K_\bullet(X)\) and in \(K_\bullet(X_s)\), whence \(\sigma_\bullet(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(Y_t))=\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(Y_s)\). One easily checks (thanks to the fact that \(X\) is catenary, hence \(Y\) catenary) that \(\dim Y_s\leq\dim Y_t\); in the case where \(f\) is of finite type, this follows at once, by reduction to the case \(X\) integral, from EGA IV 7.1.13. Whence the announced conclusion.
7.5. "Conservation of number". Suppose now \(f\) proper, hence \(X_t\) and \(X_s\) proper over \(k(t)\) resp. \(k(s)\), so that we have homomorphisms 2.3:
\[\chi_t:K_\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z,\qquad \chi_s:K_\bullet(X_s)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z.\]
This being said, we have the relation
\[(7.5.1)\qquad \chi_s(\sigma_\bullet(x))=\chi_t(x)\qquad\text{pour }x\in K_\bullet(X_t)\]
("theorem of conservation of number"), in other words, we have commutativity in the diagram
\[(7.5.2)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} K_\bullet(X_t)&\xrightarrow{\sigma_\bullet}&K_\bullet(X_s)\\ &\searrow_{\chi_t}\quad\swarrow_{\chi_s}&\\ &\mathbb Z& \end{array}\]

To prove (7.5.1), we are again reduced to the case where \(x=\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(Y_t)\), \(Y_t\) an integral closed subscheme of \(X_t\), and with the notations of the proof of 7.4 we are reduced to proving that
\[\chi(Y_s)=\chi(Y_t),\]
which is well known (EGA III 7.9.4, or Exposé III 5.7).
7.6. It is immediate that we have
\[(7.6.1)\qquad \sigma_\bullet(\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_{X_t}))=\operatorname{cl}_\bullet(\mathcal O_{X_s}),\]
which implies, taking into account that \(\sigma_\bullet\) is \(K'(X)\)-linear, that the following diagram is commutative:

\[(7.6.2)\qquad \begin{array}{ccccc} &K'(X)&&\\ {}^{j^*}\swarrow&&\searrow^{i^*}&&\\ K'(X_t)&&&&K'(X_s)\\ \downarrow&&&&\downarrow\\ K_\bullet(X_t)&&\xrightarrow{\sigma_\bullet}&&K_\bullet(X_s) \end{array}\]
where the vertical arrows are the canonical homomorphisms \(K'\longrightarrow K_\bullet\) (IV 2.5.1).

7.7. Specialization for \(K'\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet\). When \(X_t\) and \(X_s\) are regular (for example if \(f\) is smooth), the two preceding vertical arrows are isomorphisms (IV 2.5), and the specialization homomorphism can then be interpreted as a homomorphism
\[(7.7.1)\qquad \sigma^\bullet:K'(X_t)\longrightarrow K'(X_s),\]
which can again be described as the unique homomorphism making the following diagram commutative
\[(7.7.2)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} K'(X)&\xrightarrow{j^*}&K'(X_t)\\ {scriptstyle i^*}\downarrow&\swarrow{\scriptstyle\sigma^\bullet}&\\ K'(X_s)&& \end{array}\]

Since in the latter \(i^*\) and \(j^*\) are ring homomorphisms (IV 2.7 b) with \(j^*\) surjective, it follows that the specialization homomorphism (7.7.1) is a ring homomorphism. For the same reason, if \(X\) is separated, the homomorphism (7.7.1) is even a homomorphism of \(\lambda\)-rings. To make this point precise, it need only be noted that a regular separated noetherian scheme is divisorial (II 2.2.7.1), so its \(K'\) is identified with its "naive" \(K'\) (IV 2.9 (ii)), which is naturally equipped with a structure of \(\lambda\)-ring (VI 3.2). On the other hand, the fact that \(X_t\) and \(X_s\) are regular implies the same fact for \(X\) (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 17.1.8), and the homomorphisms \(i^*:K'(X)\longrightarrow K'(X_s)\) and \(j^*:K'(X)\longrightarrow K'(X_t)\) are \(\lambda\)-homomorphisms, hence the same is true of (7.7.1), which is deduced from \(i^*\) by passage to the quotient.

Note also that, \(X\) being regular hence normal, its connected components are identical with its irreducible components, which dominate \(S\), so that the homomorphism \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\longrightarrow H^0(X_t,\mathbb Z)\) induced by the inclusion \(X_t\longrightarrow X\) is bijective. Consequently the homomorphism \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\longrightarrow H^0(X_s,\mathbb Z)\) induced by the inclusion \(X_s\longrightarrow X\) can also be interpreted as a homomorphism
\[(7.7.3)\qquad H^0(X_t,\mathbb Z)\longrightarrow H^0(X_s,\mathbb Z),\]

which again deserves the name of "specialization homomorphism". This being so, one verifies immediately from the definitions that the homomorphism (7.7.1) is compatible with the structures of augmented algebras over \(H^0(X_t,\mathbb Z)\) resp. \(H^0(X_s,\mathbb Z)\) on the two sides defined in (IV 2.8), taking into account the preceding homomorphism (7.7.3).
Still assuming \(X\) separated, the above implies in particular that (7.7.1) induces a homomorphism of graded rings
\[(7.7.4)\qquad \sigma^\bullet:\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_s),\]
and using the notion of Chern class of (V 6), we find a commutative diagram

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} K'(X_t)&\xrightarrow{c_{X_t}}&\operatorname{Ch}\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_t)\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\\ K'(X_s)&\xrightarrow{c_{X_s}}&\operatorname{Ch}\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_s). \end{array}\]
7.8. Specialization for \(\operatorname{Pic}\). Let us keep the hypotheses of 7.7: \(X\) separated, \(X_t\) and \(X_s\) regular. Taking into account the canonical isomorphism (5.3.2):
\[\operatorname{Gr}^1\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Pic},\]
the homomorphism (7.7.4) thus gives us a canonical homomorphism
\[(7.8.1)\qquad \operatorname{Pic}(X_t)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}(X_s).\]

An equivalent way of describing the latter is the following: the hypothesis that \(X\) is regular implies that its local rings are factorial (by the Auslander-Buchsbaum theorem EGA IV 21.11.1), hence that every invertible sheaf \(L_t\) on \(X_t\) can be extended to an invertible sheaf \(L\) on \(X\) (EGA IV 21.6.11). This being so, one checks at once that the image under (7.8.1) of \(\operatorname{cl}(L_t)\in\operatorname{Pic}(X_t)\) is \(\operatorname{cl}(L_s)\in\operatorname{Pic}(X_s)\), where we evidently set \(L_s=L|X_s\).

In fact, using only the hypothesis that the local rings of \(X\) are factorial and that \(X_s\) is locally integral (to the exclusion of regularity and separation hypotheses), one shows that \(\operatorname{cl}(L_s)\) does not depend on the chosen extension \(L\) of \(L_t\) to an invertible Module on \(X\), so that one thus obtains directly a homomorphism (7.8.1). To verify the announced independence, we are reduced to proving that if \(L_t\simeq\mathcal O_{X_t}\), then \(L_s\simeq\mathcal O_{X_s}\). Now the hypothesis means that \(L\simeq\mathcal O_X(D)\), \(D\) a divisor on \(X\) with support \(\subset X_s\) (cf. EGA IV 21.2.11), and we reduce at once to the case \(X_s\) integral, hence \(D=nX_s\), but then \(D=\operatorname{div}(g^n)\) (\(g\) a uniformizer of \(A\)), hence \(\mathcal O_X(D)\simeq\mathcal O_X\) and a fortiori \(L_s\simeq\mathcal O_{X_s}\).
7.9. Specialization and numerical equivalence. Suppose \(f\) proper, and \(X_s\) and \(X_t\) regular, and consider the two specialization maps (7.4.1) and (7.7.4):

\[(7.9.1)\qquad \begin{aligned} \sigma_\bullet &: \operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_s),\\ \sigma^\bullet &: \operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_s). \end{aligned}\]
From the fact that the homomorphism
\[(7.9.2)\qquad \sigma^\bullet:K'(X_t)\longrightarrow K'(X_s)\]
from which they are deduced is compatible with the ring structures, and thanks to (7.5.1), we find that we have
\[(7.9.3)\qquad \langle\sigma_\bullet(x_t),\sigma^\bullet(y_t)\rangle=\langle x_t,y_t\rangle\qquad\text{pour }x_t\in\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_t),\ y_t\in\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_t),\]

using the pairings \(\langle x,y\rangle\) of 4.1 between \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet\). From this one deduces formally that if \(x\) is an element of \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_t)\) (resp. of \(\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_t)\)) such that \(\sigma_\bullet(x)\) (resp. \(\sigma^\bullet(x)\)) is numerically equivalent to zero (4.5.1), then the same is true of \(x\). The converse is true if (7.9.2) (or what amounts to the same thing, one or the other homomorphism (7.9.1) deduced from it by passage to the associated graded groups) is an epimorphism mod. torsion, — a circumstance which is however realized only rather exceptionally.

7.9.4. It is plausible, at least when \(f\) is proper and smooth, that the contemplated converse is always valid, i.e. that \(\sigma_\bullet(x)\) (resp. \(\sigma^\bullet(x)\)) is numerically equivalent to zero as soon as this is the case for \(x\), which would imply that the homomorphisms (7.9.1) induce monomorphisms for the corresponding quotient groups by the numerical equivalence relation \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet^{\mathrm{num}}\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{num}}^\bullet\). At least the above implies that the group \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet^{\mathrm{num}}(X_t)\) is isomorphic to a quotient of a subgroup of \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet^{\mathrm{num}}(X_s)\) (and in particular, it has rank at most equal to that of \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet^{\mathrm{num}}(X_s)\)), and similarly for \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{num}}^\bullet\), and also for \(K_{\mathrm{num}}'\) itself.
7.10. Compatibility with direct images and inverse images
Let us give ourselves a morphism
\[g:X\longrightarrow Y\]
of noetherian schemes flat over \(S\). We denote by \(\sigma_X\) (resp. \(\sigma_Y\)) the specialization homomorphism for \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)), and by \(i_X,j_X\) (resp. \(i_Y,j_Y\))
the morphisms \(i,j\) for \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) of the special and general fibers into the ambient scheme. We denote by
\[g_t:X_t\longrightarrow Y_t,\qquad g_s:X_s\longrightarrow Y_s\]
the induced morphisms, thus giving rise to the cartesian squares

\[(7.10.0)\qquad \begin{array}{ccccc} X_t&\xrightarrow{g_t}&Y_t&&X_s\xrightarrow{g_s}Y_s\\ {\scriptstyle j_X}\downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle j_Y}&&{\scriptstyle i_X}\downarrow\qquad\downarrow{\scriptstyle i_Y}\\ X&\xrightarrow{g}&Y&&X\xrightarrow{g}Y. \end{array}\]
Suppose first \(g\) proper. I claim that then the following diagram is commutative:

\[(7.10.1)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} K_\bullet(X_t)&\xrightarrow{g_{t*}}&K_\bullet(Y_t)\\ {\scriptstyle\sigma_X}\downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle\sigma_Y}\\ K_\bullet(X_s)&\xrightarrow{g_{s*}}&K_\bullet(Y_s). \end{array}\]

This result generalizes 7.5 (since for \(X=S\), the arrows of (7.3.2) are identified with the identity arrow of \(\mathbb Z\)). Note that if \(X\) and \(Y\) satisfy the conditions of 7.7, i.e. have regular fibers, then \(g_t\) and \(g_s\) are automatically of finite tor-dimension, and the diagram can be interpreted as a commutative diagram with \(K'\), which we leave to the reader to rewrite.
To prove the commutativity of (7.10.1), one sees at once, using the definition of \(\sigma_X\) and \(\sigma_Y\), that we are reduced to proving the commutativity of the two front squares of the prism

\[(*)\qquad \begin{array}{ccccc} K_\bullet(X_t)&\xrightarrow{g_{t*}}&K_\bullet(Y_t)\\ \uparrow{\scriptstyle j_X^*}&&\uparrow{\scriptstyle j_Y^*}\\[-2pt] K_\bullet(X)&\xrightarrow{g_*}&K_\bullet(Y)\\ {\scriptstyle i_X^*}\downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle i_Y^*}\\ K_\bullet(X_s)&\xrightarrow{g_{s*}}&K_\bullet(Y_s). \end{array}\]

Now these two squares are the "base change" squares deduced from the two squares (7.10.0), so that we can apply (IV 3 ). It is only necessary to check that these squares correspond to pairs \((X,Y_t)\) and \((X,Y_s)\) of schemes over \(Y\) which are tor-independent over \(Y\). This is trivial for the first pair, \(Y_t\longrightarrow Y\) being flat, and also immediate for the second, the immersion \(Y_s\longrightarrow Y\) being regular of codimension 1 and remaining so after the base change \(X\longrightarrow Y\).

Let us now no longer assume \(g\) proper, but assume instead that it is of finite tor-dimension. I claim that the same is then true of \(g_t\) and \(g_s\). This is trivial for the first, and for the second it follows from the fact (which we have just pointed out) that \(X\) and \(Y_s\) are tor-independent over \(Y\). We can therefore consider the square of homomorphisms

\[(7.10.2)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} K_\bullet(Y_t)&\xrightarrow{g_t^*}&K_\bullet(X_t)\\ {\scriptstyle\sigma_Y}\downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle\sigma_X}\\ K_\bullet(Y_s)&\xrightarrow{g_s^*}&K_\bullet(X_s), \end{array}\]

where the horizontal arrows are defined in (IV 2.12). Note that if \(X\) and \(Y\) satisfy the conditions of 7.7, i.e. have regular fibers, then (\(Y\) being regular) \(g\) is automatically of finite tor-dimension, and the preceding diagram can be interpreted as the commutativity of a diagram of rings \(K'\).

To prove the commutativity of (7.10.2), it again suffices to prove that of the front squares of the prism analogous to (*), but where the direct image homomorphisms \(g_{t*},g_*,g_{s*}\) are replaced by the inverse image homomorphisms \(g_t^*,g^*,g_s^*\). Now the commutativity of these squares follows at
