once from that of the corresponding squares (7.10.0), taking into account the transitivity of inverse images (IV 2.12).
Remark 7.10.3. When \(g\) is proper, the commutativity of (7.10.1) at once implies that of the following diagram, which is deduced from it by passage to the associated graded objects:

\[(7.10.1\ \mathrm{bis})\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} \operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_t)&\xrightarrow{g_{t*}}&\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(Y_t)\\ {\scriptstyle\sigma_{X\bullet}}\downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle\sigma_{Y\bullet}}\\ \operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_s)&\xrightarrow{g_{s*}}&\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(Y_s). \end{array}\]
Similarly, if \(X\) and \(Y\) have regular fibers and are separated, the commutativity of (7.10.2) implies that of

\[(7.10.2\ \mathrm{bis})\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} \operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(Y_t)&\xrightarrow{g_t^*}&\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_t)\\ {\scriptstyle\sigma_Y^\bullet}\downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle\sigma_X^\bullet}\\ \operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(Y_s)&\xrightarrow{g_s^*}&\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(X_s). \end{array}\]

When \(X\) and \(Y\) are moreover quasi-projective and smooth over \(S\), it is known besides that \(g_t^*\) and \(g_s^*\) are compatible with the filtrations by codimension of supports, and one finds a commutative diagram like (7.10.2 bis), but with the \(\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet\) replaced by the \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}^\bullet\) (cf. Exp. 0, 2.1). One may also note that if \(X\) and \(Y\) are quasi-projective over \(S\) with regular fibers and separated, then \(f_t\) and \(f_s\) are automatically complete intersection morphisms (VIII 1.1), and one obtains a commutative diagram analogous to (7.10.1 bis), where the \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet\) are replaced by the \(\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(')_{\mathbb Q}\), taking into account the compatibility result (VIII 3.2).
7.11. For a scheme \(T\) over an algebraically closed field \(k\), one can define the subgroup of \(K'(T)\) of elements algebraically equivalent to zero as the subgroup of elements of the form
\[(7.11.1)\qquad E(x_1)-E(x_0),\]

where \(x_0\) and \(x_1\) are two \(k\)-rational points of an algebraic curve \(C\) proper, regular and connected over \(k\), \(E\) an element of \(K'(T\mathbin{\times_k}C)\), and where for every point \(x\) rational over \(k\) of \(C\), we set
\[E(x)=i_x^*(E)\in K'(T),\quad\text{où }i_x:T\longrightarrow T\mathbin{\times_k}C\]

is the morphism \(t\longmapsto(t,x)\) defined by \(x\). The fact that the elements of the form (7.11.1) form a subgroup of \(K'(T)\) is seen by a standard argument, using a product \(C\mathbin{\times_k}C'\) and the pair of points \((x_1,x'_1)\), \((x_0,x'_0)\) of it, and the fact that through two points of an irreducible quasi-projective algebraic scheme over \(k\) (here \(C\mathbin{\times_k}C'\)) one can always pass an irreducible algebraic curve. (The reader who does not wish to admit this fact may define algebraic equivalence by the group generated by the elements of the form (7.11.1)). The elements algebraically equivalent to zero form an ideal of \(K'(T)\), as one sees at once, which is moreover stable under the operations \(\lambda^i\) when these are defined, i.e. when one works with the "naive" \(K'\); this ideal is moreover contained in the augmentation ideal of \(K'(T)\), as one checks trivially. We can therefore define a quotient ring
\[(7.11.2)\qquad K'_{\mathrm{alg}}(T),\]
which is even a \(\lambda\)-ring when one works with the naive \(K'\).
One can define in the same way a subgroup of elements algebraically equivalent to 0 in \(K_\bullet(T)\), by taking above \(E\) in \(K_\bullet(T\mathbin{\times_k}C)\), and

noting that \(i_x:T\longrightarrow T\mathbin{\times_k}C\) is a perfect morphism, since it is deduced by the flat base change \(T\mathbin{\times_k}C\longrightarrow C\) from the morphism \(j_x:x\longrightarrow C\), which is perfect thanks to the hypothesis that \(C\) is regular. One thus finds a quotient group
\[(7.11.3)\qquad K_\bullet^{\mathrm{alg}}(T)\]

of \(K_\bullet(T)\). The latter is moreover in a natural way a module over \(K'_{\mathrm{alg}}(T)\), for one sees at once from the definitions that the product of an element of \(K'(T)\) by an element of \(K_\bullet(T)\) is algebraically equivalent to zero if one of the two factors is, using (IV 2.12.2) for \(i_x\).

One defines (when working with the naive \(K'\)) the ideal of \(\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(T)\) of elements algebraically equivalent to zero as being the ideal generated by the \(c^i(x)\), where \(i\geq1\) and \(x\in K'(T)\) is algebraically equivalent to zero; the quotient graded ring
\[(7.11.4)\qquad \operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{alg}}^\bullet(T)\]
is then none other than the Chern ring associated with the \(\lambda\)-ring \(K'_{\mathrm{alg}}(T)\).

Finally one defines the graded subgroup of \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(T)\) of elements algebraically equivalent to zero (\(T\) being assumed locally noetherian), as the one whose component of dimension \(i\) is the image in \(\operatorname{Gr}_i(T)\) of the subgroup of \(\operatorname{Fil}_iK_\bullet(T)\) formed of the elements algebraically equivalent to zero in \(K_\bullet(T)\). One finds at once that the quotient graded group
\[(7.11.5)\qquad \operatorname{Gr}^{\mathrm{alg}}_\bullet(T)\]

is a module over the ring \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{alg}}^\bullet(T)\), by passage to the quotient from the module structure of \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(T)\) over \(\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(T)\) (3.1).

7.11.6. The preceding definitions would keep a meaning if one does not assume \(k\) algebraically closed, by taking above \(C\) geometrically connected over \(k\). It is not clear however that these definitions would be very useful; in particular, it does not seem that in the case of the group \(\operatorname{Pic}(T)\), one

recovers the classical "geometric" notion of algebraic equivalence contemplated in 2.4.1. So we prefer to say that an element of \(K'(T)\) is algebraically equivalent to zero if its image in \(K'(T\otimes_k\bar k)\) is, where \(\bar k\) is an algebraic closure of \(k\), and to proceed in the same way for the definition of algebraic equivalence in the groups \(K_\bullet(T)\), \(\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(T)\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(T)\).

7.12. These definitions being laid down, I claim that the specialization homomorphism (7.3.1) transforms elements algebraically equivalent to zero into elements algebraically equivalent to zero, and consequently defines by passage to the quotient a homomorphism
\[(7.12.1)\qquad K_\bullet^{\mathrm{alg}}(X_t)\longrightarrow K_\bullet^{\mathrm{alg}}(X_s).\]

To see this, we reduce immediately to the case where \(A\) is complete and \(k(s)\) algebraically closed (by means of an easy compatibility made explicit in 7.15). It suffices to see that \(\sigma\) transforms an element of the form (7.11.1) of \(K_\bullet(X_t)\) into an element algebraically equivalent to zero in \(K_\bullet(X_s)\). Here \(T=X_t\), and we will accordingly write \(C_t\), \(E_t\) instead of \(C\), \(E\) as above. We know that \(C_t\) is projective over \(k(t)\), hence (EGA IV 2.8.5) there exists a projective and flat scheme \(C\) over \(S\) whose generic fiber is \(C_t\). Using Abhyankar's resolution of singularities of arithmetic surfaces [6], one sees that one can even choose for \(C\) a regular scheme. (N.B. Flatness is preserved by resolution, thanks to EGA IV 14.3.8). Since \(C\) is proper over \(S\), the points \(x_{0t}\) and \(x_{1t}\) of \(C_t\) rational over \(k(t)\) extend to sections \(x_0\), \(x_1\) of \(C\) over \(S\), and since \(C\) is regular, it is well known that \(C\) is smooth over \(S\) at the points of \(x_0(s)\), \(x_1(s)\) (EGA IV, 17.12.1 c) \(\Rightarrow\) a) and 19.1.1). It

follows that the immersions \(x_i:S\longrightarrow X\) (\(i=0,1\)) are regular, and remain regular immersions \(x_i:S\longrightarrow C\) under the base change \(s\longrightarrow S\). Note that by virtue of the homotopy exact sequence (IX 1.1), \(E_t\in K_\bullet(X_t\mathbin{\times_{k(t)}}C_t)\) comes from an element \(E\in K_\bullet(X\mathbin{\times_S}C)\), hence the element \(E_t(x_{1t})-E_t(x_{0t})\) of \(K_\bullet(X_t)\) comes from the element \(E(x_1)-E(x_0)\) of \(K_\bullet(X\mathbin{\times_S}C)\), and consequently its image in \(K_\bullet(X_s)\) under (7.3.1) is none other than
\[(*)\qquad i^*(E(x_1)-E(x_0)).\]
Of course, for a section \(x\) of \(C\) over \(S\), we set
\[E(x)=i_x^*(E),\quad\text{où }i_x:X\longrightarrow X\mathbin{\times_S}C\]

is defined as above, and can be regarded as deduced from \(x\) by the flat base change \(X\mathbin{\times_S}C\longrightarrow C\); it is therefore a regular immersion, a fortiori a perfect morphism, which indeed gives a meaning to the expression \(E(x)\). For the same reason the expression \(E_s(x_s)\) is defined, and by transitivity of inverse images (IV 2.12) in the following commutative diagram of perfect morphisms:
\[ \begin{array}{ccc} X&\xrightarrow{i_x}&X\mathbin{\times_S}C\\ {\scriptstyle i}\uparrow&&\uparrow\\ X_s&\xrightarrow{i_{x_s}}&X_s\mathbin{\times_{k(s)}}C_s, \end{array} \]
one finds that the left-hand side of (*) is equal to
\[(**)\qquad E_s(x_{1s})-E_s(x_{0s}).\]

Since \(k(s)\) is algebraically closed, the reduced irreducible components of \(C_s\) are geometrically integral over \(k(s)\) and their intersection points rational over \(k(s)\). One easily concludes that the expression (**) is algebraically equivalent to zero in \(K_\bullet(X_s)\), using the connectedness
of \(C_s\), which follows from the geometric connectedness of \(C_t\).
From the homomorphism (7.12.1) one deduces thanks to 7.4 a homomorphism
\[(7.12.2)\qquad \operatorname{Gr}^{\mathrm{alg}}_\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Gr}^{\mathrm{alg}}_\bullet(X_s).\]
When one assumes moreover \(X_s\) and \(X_t\) regular, then (7.12.1) can again be interpreted as a ring homomorphism
\[(7.12.3)\qquad K'_{\mathrm{alg}}(X_t)\longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{alg}}(X_s),\]
which, when \(X\) is moreover separated, is a homomorphism of \(\lambda\)-rings, and consequently defines a homomorphism
\[(7.12.4)\qquad \operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{alg}}^\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{alg}}^\bullet(X_s).\]

7.12.5. Let \(T\) be an algebraic scheme smooth and proper over a field \(k\). One easily checks that an element of \(K'(T)\) such that there exists an integer \(n\geq1\) with \(nx\) algebraically equivalent to zero, is numerically equivalent to zero. It is plausible that the converse is always true, at least if \(T\) is projective. In this question, one may obviously assume \(k\) algebraically closed, and replace \(K'(T)\) by \(\operatorname{Gr}^\bullet(T)\) or \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(T)\), without modifying the conjecture. One also finds an equivalent conjecture by working with the Chow ring \(A(T)\) (cf. XIV 4.1), and one finds the following more or less classical conjecture: if \(Z\) is an algebraic cycle numerically equivalent to zero on \(T\), there exists an integer \(n\geq1\) with \(nZ\) algebraically equivalent to zero. In the case where this conjecture is correct for \(X_t\), it would follow from the compatibility result of the homomorphism (7.3.1) with algebraic equivalence that this specialization homomorphism (in the case where \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) is a projective and smooth morphism) is also compatible with numerical equivalence, as conjectured in 7.9.
7.13. Relations with \(\ell\)-adic cohomology

Recall (SGA 5 VII) that for every locally free Module of finite type \(E\) on a scheme \(T\), and every prime number distinct from the residue characteristics of \(T\), one defines \(\ell\)-adic Chern classes (for \(i\geq0\))
\[c^i(E)\in H^{2i}(T,\mathbb Z_\ell(i)),\]
satisfying an additivity formula
\[c(E)=c(E')c(E'')\]

when \(E\) is an extension of a locally free module \(E'\) by a locally free module \(E''\). From this additivity one concludes that the total Chern class \(c(E)=\sum_i c^i(E)\) comes from a homomorphism
\[(7.13.0)\qquad c:K'(T)\longrightarrow \widehat G(H^{2*}(T,\mathbb Z_\ell(*)))=1+\prod_{i\geq1}H^{2i}(T,\mathbb Z_\ell(i)),\]
(where the target object is a group under the product, as explained in V 1, and where the \(K'(T)\) is the "naive" \(K'\)). From this one deduces canonical maps
\[(7.13.1)\qquad c^i:K'(T)\longrightarrow H^{2i}(T,\mathbb Z_\ell(i)).\]

This being so, let \(X\) be a scheme proper and flat over \(S\), with regular fibers, and suppose \(S\) henselian. We assume \(\ell\neq\operatorname{car.}\) residual of \(S\). We can then form the diagram

\[(7.13.2)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} K'(X_t)&\xrightarrow{c^i}&H^{2i}(X_t,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\\ {\scriptstyle\sigma}\downarrow&&\uparrow{\scriptstyle\sigma^{-1}}\\ K'(X_s)&\xrightarrow{c^i}&H^{2i}(X_s,\mathbb Z_\ell(i)), \end{array} \]
where the horizontal arrows are the "Chern class" maps relative to \(X_t\) and \(X_s\) respectively, and where \(\sigma^{-1}\) is the composite homomorphism
\[H(X_s)\xleftarrow{i^*}H(X)\xrightarrow{j^*}H(X_t)\ ;\]

the arrows written here are restriction homomorphisms, and one uses the fact that \(i^*\) is an isomorphism by the proper base change theorem (SGA 4 XII 5.5). I claim that the square (7.13.2) is commutative. The proof is essentially the same as the one establishing the commutativity of (7.10.2); it suffices here to use the compatibility of the formation of Chern classes with inverse images, in the case of the morphisms \(j:X_t\longrightarrow X\) and \(i:X_s\longrightarrow X\).

7.13.3. Note that the square (7.13.2) comes by passage to the projective limit from the analogous squares, where cohomology with coefficients in the \(\ell\)-adic sheaf \(\mathbb Z_\ell(i)\) is replaced by that with coefficients in the torsion sheaves \(\mu_n^{\otimes i}\) (where \(n\) runs through the powers of \(\ell\)). Hence the commutativity of (7.13.2) merely expresses the commutativity of the squares involving the Chern classes mod. \(n\), for every power \(n\) of \(\ell\). Let us choose an algebraic closure \(\bar K\) of \(K=k(t)\), whence an algebraic closure \(\bar k\) of \(k=k(s)\) (namely the residue field of the normal closure of \(V\) in \(\bar K\)). For every finite subextension \(K_\alpha\) of \(\bar K\), one can consider the analogous commutative diagram (for fixed \(n\)) relative to the situation deduced from \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) by the base change \(S_\alpha\longrightarrow S\), \(S_\alpha\) being the normalization of \(S\) in \(K_\alpha\). For this it should be noted that, by Krull-Akizuki, and \(S\) being henselian, \(S_\alpha\) is indeed a trait; moreover, it will be necessary to assume here that the fibers of \(X\) over \(S\) are geometrically regular, i.e. \(X\) smooth over \(S\), so that this hypothesis is preserved by base change. One thus finds, for variable \(\alpha\), an inductive system of commutative squares. Passing to the inductive limit and using (SGA 4 VII 5.8) and (IV 3.2 ), one finds a commutative square (cf. 7.16):

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} K'(X_{\bar t})&\xrightarrow{c^i}&H^{2i}(X_{\bar t},\mu_n^{\otimes i})\\ {\scriptstyle\sigma}\downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle\sigma^*}\\ K'(X_{\bar s})&\xrightarrow{c^i}&H^{2i}(X_{\bar s},\mu_n^{\otimes i}), \end{array} \]

where \(X_{\bar t}\), \(X_{\bar s}\) are the geometric fibers of \(X\). Moreover, it is known that now the specialization homomorphism \(\sigma^*\) for cohomology is an isomorphism (SGA 4 XVI 2.2), so the commutativity of the preceding square can also be expressed by that of the square deduced from it by inverting the preceding arrow. Then passing to the projective limit over \(n\) in the commutative squares thus obtained, one proves a commutative square

\[(7.13.4)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} K'(X_{\bar t})&\longrightarrow&H^{2i}(X_{\bar t},\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\\ {\scriptstyle\sigma}\downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle\sim}\\ K'(X_{\bar s})&\longrightarrow&H^{2i}(X_{\bar s},\mathbb Z_\ell(i)). \end{array} \]

7.13.5. Another way of expressing this commutativity is to say that, if \(x_{\bar t}\) is an element of \(K'(X_{\bar t})\) and \(x_{\bar s}\) its image in \(K'(X_{\bar s})\), then \(c^i(x_{\bar t})\) and \(c^i(x_{\bar s})\) are the values at the geometric points \(\bar t\) and \(\bar s\) respectively, of a section of \(R^{2i}f_*(\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\). This section is moreover easily interpreted, by taking an element \(x\in K'(X)\) which induces \(x_{\bar t}\), whence a \(c^i(x)\in H^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\), which defines the desired section of \(R^{2i}f_*(\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\).
7.13.6. Let \(T\) be a scheme. Then by passage to the associated graded objects, the homomorphism (7.13.0) defines homomorphisms
\[\psi_i:\operatorname{Gr}^i(T)\longrightarrow H^{2i}(T,\mathbb Z_\ell(i)),\]
the image under \(\psi_i\) of an element \(x\) of \(\operatorname{Filt}^iK'(T)\) being by definition \(c^i(x)\). (The fact that (7.13.0) is compatible with the \(\lambda\)-filtration on

\(K'(T)\) and the filtration by degree on the right-hand side follows easily from (V 6.6.4) and from the fact that the \(c^i\) define a Chern theory of \(K'(T)\) in the sense of (V 6.13), when one regards \(K'(T)\) as an augmented \(\lambda\)-algebra over the binomial ring \(H^0(T,\mathbb Z)\)). From this one deduces maps

\[(7.13.7)\qquad c\ell:\operatorname{Gr}^i(T)_{\mathbb Q}\longrightarrow H^{2i}(T,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\mathrel{\mathop{=}^{\mathrm{dfn}}}H^{2i}(T,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell,\]
by the formula
\[(7.13.8)\qquad c\ell(x^{(i)})=(1/(-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!)\,\psi_i(x^{(i)}),\]
compare (XIV 5.1 and 5.2). This being so, the commutativity of the diagrams (7.13.2) and (7.13.4) trivially implies that of the analogous diagrams

\[(7.13.9)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} \operatorname{Gr}^i(X_t)_{\mathbb Q}&\xrightarrow{c\ell}&H^{2i}(X_t,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\\ {\scriptstyle\sigma_{\mathbb Q}}\downarrow&&\uparrow{\scriptstyle\sigma^{-1}}\\ \operatorname{Gr}^i(X_s)_{\mathbb Q}&\xrightarrow{c\ell}&H^{2i}(X_s,\mathbb Q_\ell(i)), \end{array} \]

\[(7.13.10)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} \operatorname{Gr}^i(X_{\bar t})_{\mathbb Q}&\xrightarrow{c\ell}&H^{2i}(X_{\bar t},\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle\sim}\\ \operatorname{Gr}^i(X_{\bar s})_{\mathbb Q}&\xrightarrow{c\ell}&H^{2i}(X_{\bar s},\mathbb Q_\ell(i)). \end{array} \]

Remark 7.13.11. The commutativity of the last two diagrams, which can be expressed by saying that the specialization of algebraic cycles is compatible with the formation of the associated \(\ell\)-adic cohomology classes, is not very satisfactory in the stated form, since we were obliged to replace the coefficients \(\mathbb Z_\ell(i)\) by \(\mathbb Q_\ell(i)\), that is to say to neglect torsion. To see a statement which does not neglect torsion, it seems that one

will be obliged to work with the Chow ring, since it is on the latter that the homomorphism \(c\ell\) is naturally defined as a homomorphism with values in \(H^{2*}(-,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\), compare (XIV 4.1). Unfortunately, no theory of specialization for the Chow ring (cf. 7.14) has been developed for the moment, which would allow one to write commutative squares analogous to (7.13.9) and (7.13.10) in terms of the Chow rings and of cohomology with coefficients \(\mathbb Z_\ell(*)\). All that can be said when \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) is smooth and proper is that we have a commutative square

\[(7.13.12)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} Z^i(X_t)&\xrightarrow{c\ell}&H^{2i}(X_t,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\\ {\scriptstyle\sigma}\downarrow&&\uparrow\\ Z^i(X_s)&\xrightarrow{c\ell}&H^{2i}(X_s,\mathbb Z_\ell(i)), \end{array} \]

analogous to (7.13.9), and a corresponding square analogous to (7.13.10) which we leave to the reader to write, where \(Z^i\) denotes the group of algebraic cycles (modulo nothing at all) of codimension \(i\), where the horizontal arrows are the "associated cohomology class" homomorphisms defined in (SGA 5 IV), and where \(\sigma\) denotes the specialization homomorphism of algebraic cycles, associating to a cycle \(Z_t\) the cycle \(Z_s\), where \(Z\) is the schematic closure of \(Z_t\) in \(X\) (cf. 7.15)). This commutativity follows from the following fact, established in (SGA 5 IV); if \(Z\) is a closed subscheme of \(X\) of codimension \(\geq i\) on each fiber, one can associate to it a cohomology class \(c\ell(Z)\in H^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\), whose formation commutes with any base change, and in particular such that its restriction to \(X_t\) (resp. \(X_s\)) is equal to \(c\ell(Z_t)\) (resp. \(c\ell(Z_s)\)). Of course, when one knows that the homomorphism \(\sigma\) in (7.13.12) transforms cycles rationally equivalent to zero into cycles rationally equivalent to zero (\(X\) being projective over \(S\)),
one can in this diagram replace the \(Z^i\) by the \(A^i\), and one finds a diagram of the desired type, in terms of the Chow rings of \(X_t\) and \(X_s\).

Remark 7.13.13. Let \(T\) be a smooth projective scheme over an algebraically closed field. It follows trivially from the formalism developed in (SGA 5 IV) that if \(Z\) is a cycle of codimension \(i\) on \(T\) whose image in \(H^{2i}(T,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\) is zero, then \(Z\) is numerically equivalent to zero. It is one of the standard conjectures of the theory of algebraic cycles that the converse is also true. It is moreover immediate, thanks to (VII 4.11), that one finds an equivalent conjecture by replacing there the group of algebraic cycles by the group \(\operatorname{Gr}^i(T)_{\mathbb Q}\), using the definition (7.13.8), or again by the group \(K'(T)\), by writing that numerical equivalence to zero of an element of \(K'(T)\) is equivalent to the vanishing of its augmentation and of its Chern classes in the \(H^{2i}(T,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\).

This being so, it follows immediately from the commutativity of (7.13.10) that if \(X_{\bar t}\) satisfies the preceding conjecture, then the specialization homomorphism \(\operatorname{Gr}^i(X_{\bar t})\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^i(X_{\bar s})\) (and a fortiori \(\operatorname{Gr}^i(X_t)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^i(X_s)\)) is compatible with numerical equivalence, as was conjectured in 7.9. It will be noted moreover that the conjecture appealed to for this (numerical equivalence implies \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-cohomological equivalence) is weaker than the one contemplated in 7.11.5.
7.14. Specialization homomorphism for the groups of cycles and classes of cycles.

If \(T\) is a noetherian scheme, let us denote by \(Z_i(T)\) (resp. \(Z^i(T)\)) the group of algebraic cycles on \(T\) purely of dimension \(i\) (resp. purely of codimension \(i\)). When \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) is of finite type, one then defines
a specialization homomorphism:
\[(7.14.1)\qquad \sigma_i:Z_i(X_t)\longrightarrow Z_i(X_s),\]

by defining, for every prime cycle \(Z_t\) of \(X_t\), purely of dimension \(i\), identified with an integral subscheme of \(X_t\), the element \(\sigma_i(Z_t)\) of \(Z_i(X_s)\) as the cycle associated with the closed subscheme \(Z_s\) of \(X_s\), where \(Z\) is the schematic closure of \(Z_t\) in \(X\) (which is a closed subscheme of \(X\) flat over \(S\) (EGA IV 2.8), with generic fiber equal to the initial \(Z_t\), and satisfying \(\dim Z_s=\dim Z_t\) (EGA IV 7.1.13)). When \(X_s\) is locally equidimensional (i.e. all its irreducible components passing through a given point have the same dimension) one checks at once that the specialization homomorphism just defined induces homomorphisms
\[(7.14.1\ \mathrm{bis})\qquad \sigma^i:Z^i(X_t)\longrightarrow Z^i(X_s).\]

Suppose now \(f\) quasi-projective, so that one has the notion of rational equivalence of cycles on \(X_t\) and on \(X_s\) (cf. [5], where the theory is done over an algebraically closed field). It seems very plausible that the homomorphism (7.14.1) transforms cycles rationally equivalent to zero into cycles rationally equivalent to zero, and therefore induces a corresponding homomorphism on the "Chow" groups of classes of cycles
\[(7.14.2)\qquad \sigma_i:A_i(X_t)\longrightarrow A_i(X_s),\]
or again, when \(X_s\) is locally equidimensional:
\[(7.14.2\ \mathrm{bis})\qquad \sigma^i:A^i(X_t)\longrightarrow A^i(X_s).\]
One might think of proving it by a method paraphrasing that of 7.12, but where the recourse to resolution of singularities and the reduction

to the case of an algebraically closed residue field would become unnecessary, since here one has \(C=\mathbb P^1_S\) as a "parameter curve" for deforming the cycles. One runs however into a difficulty, for with the notations of 7.12, \(E\) must now denote a cycle on \(\mathbb P^1_S\times_S X\) such that \(E_t(x_{1t})-E_t(x_{0t})\) "is defined", but it by no means follows that the cycle \(E_s(x_{1s})-E_s(x_{0s})\) is too. One is therefore reduced, as was to be expected, to extracting a statement of the type of Chow's "moving lemma" in order to succeed in defining (7.14.2).

Note however that the existence of the homomorphism (7.4.1) shows that (7.14.1) is compatible with the equivalence relation on cycles defined by the surjective homomorphism \(Z_\bullet(T)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(T)\), which is only slightly coarser than linear equivalence; thus, if \(Z\) is quasi-projective and smooth over a field, we point out elsewhere that it does not differ from it up to torsion (XIV 4.1, 4.2). One therefore finds in this case a homomorphism analogous to (7.14.2), or better (7.14.2 bis), but with the two sides tensored by \(\mathbb Q\).

The most natural way to define a homomorphism (7.14.2) directly would seem to be to proceed as in 7.3. This would however require having a theory of the Chow groups \(A_i\) (or of the Chow ring in the regular case) for schemes more general than quasi-projective schemes over a field, encompassing \(X\), and a contravariant law for these groups, allowing one to define the restriction homomorphism to \(X_s\), \(A_i(X)\longrightarrow A_i(X_s)\). Compare XIV 8. Once in possession of such a sufficiently general theory of Chow groups and rings, it should be possible to transcribe into Chow theory all the results enumerated previously for the specialization homomorphism in K-theory.
Let \(S'\) be a second trait and
\[S'\longrightarrow S\]

a morphism of traits, i.e. a dominant local morphism (i.e. transforming closed point into closed point, generic point into generic point). We denote by \(s'\) (resp. \(t'\)) the closed point (resp. the generic point) of \(S'\), and we set \(X'=X\times_S S'\). Suppose \(X'\) noetherian (e.g. \(X\) of finite type over \(S\)). We have a commutative diagram
\[ \begin{array}{ccccc} X_s&\xrightarrow{i_X}&X&\xleftarrow{j_X}&X_t\\ \uparrow&&\nwarrow&&\nwarrow\\ X'_{s'}&\xrightarrow{i_{X'}}&X'&\xleftarrow{j_{X'}}&X'_{t'}, \end{array} \]

with the oblique arrows flat morphisms. We can therefore take the inverse image homomorphisms for K. by all the arrows of the diagram (IV 2.12). Applying the definition 7.3, we find that the following diagram
\[(7.15.1)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} K_\bullet(X_t)&\xrightarrow{\sigma_X}&K_\bullet(X_s)\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\\ K_\bullet(X'_{t'})&\xrightarrow{\sigma_{X'}}&K_\bullet(X'_{s'}) \end{array} \]

is commutative, where the vertical arrows are the inverse image homomorphisms. From this one deduces at once an analogous commutative diagram, with \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet\) instead of \(K_\bullet\); and when \(X_s\) and \(X_t\) are regular, one can also write \(K'\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}'\) instead of \(K_\bullet\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet\).
7.16. Passage to the geometric fibers.

Let \(\bar K\) be an algebraic closure of \(K=k(t)\), the inductive limit of its finite subextensions \(K_i\), and let \(\bar A=\varinjlim\bar A_i\) be the normalization of \(A\) in \(\bar K\), the inductive limit of its normalizations \(\bar A_i\) in the \(K_i\). Let us choose a maximal ideal \(\bar{\mathfrak m}\) of \(\bar A\), thus inducing on each \(\bar A_i\) a maximal ideal \(\bar{\mathfrak m}_i\), and let \(B_i=\bar A_{i,\bar{\mathfrak m}_i}\), which by Krull-Akizuki is again a discrete valuation ring, whose spectrum we denote by \(S_i\), and whose points by \(s_i\) and \(t_i\). The residue field \(\bar k=\bar A/\bar{\mathfrak m}\) is an algebraic closure of \(k=k(s)\), and it is the inductive limit of the residue fields \(k_i=\bar A_i/\bar{\mathfrak m}_i=k(s_i)\) of the \(B_i\). Let \(\bar t=\operatorname{Spec}(\bar K)\), \(\bar s=\operatorname{Spec}(\bar k)\). Suppose now \(X\) of finite type over \(S\), hence \(X_{\bar t}\) and \(X_{\bar s}\) of finite type over \(\bar t\) resp. \(\bar s\), hence noetherian. By virtue of IV 3.2.3, we have
\[K_\bullet(X_{\bar t})\xleftarrow{\sim}\varinjlim_i K_\bullet(X_{t_i}),\qquad K_\bullet(X_{\bar s})\xleftarrow{\sim}\varinjlim_i K_\bullet(X_{s_i}).\]

On the other hand, by virtue of 7.15 we have a homomorphism from the inductive system of the \(K_\bullet(X_{t_i})\) into the inductive system of the \(K_\bullet(X_{s_i})\). Passing to the limit, we find a canonical homomorphism:
\[(7.16.1)\qquad K_\bullet(X_{\bar t})\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_{\bar s}),\]

again called, as is fitting, the specialization homomorphism. It induces a homomorphism \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_{\bar t})\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(X_{\bar s})\) thanks to 7.4, using the fact (whose verification is left to the reader) that the functor \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet\) (just like \(K_\bullet\)) commutes with the limits which occur here. Similarly, when \(f\) is smooth, one can interpret (7.16.1) as a homomorphism on the \(K'\), which will moreover be a ring homomorphism, and even a homomorphism of \(\lambda\)-rings if \(X\) is separated; in this case, it therefore induces a homomorphism on the \(\operatorname{Gr}'\).

7.16.2. We will not review here, in the case of geometric fibers, the extension of all the properties of the specialization homomorphisms proved in the preceding sections for the "arithmetic" fibers; they are all deduced trivially from the latter by passage to the limit. Let us only point out the following interesting consequence of 7.9.4, when \(f\) is smooth and proper: \(\operatorname{Gr}^{\mathrm{num}}_\bullet(X_{\bar t})\) is isomorphic to a quotient of a subgroup of \(\operatorname{Gr}^{\mathrm{num}}_\bullet(X_{\bar s})\), in particular its rank is bounded by that of the latter group. It is therefore a semi-continuity result for the rank of the group of cycles up to numerical equivalence.
7.17. Case of an arbitrary base.
Let now
\[f:X\longrightarrow S\]

be a flat and quasi-compact morphism of noetherian preschemes, let \(s,t\) be two points of \(S\) such that \(s\) is a specialization of \(t\); we want to relate the groups K. etc for the two fibers \(X_t\) and \(X_s\), and for corresponding geometric fibers. For this, one notes (EGA II 7.1.9) that there exists a morphism
\[g:S'\longrightarrow S,\]

where \(S'\) is a trait, and where \(g\) transforms the closed point \(s'\) into \(s\), the generic point \(t'\) into \(t\); one can even assume that \(k(t)\xrightarrow{\sim}k(t')\). Suppose that \(X'=X\times_S S'\) is noetherian, which is the case if \(f\) is of finite type or if \(S'\) is essentially of finite type over \(S\) (EGA IV 1.3.8) (which can be assumed if \(S\) itself is universally Japanese (EGA 0IV 23.1.1)). Then the specialization homomorphism for \(X'\) defines a homomorphism
\[(7.17.1)\qquad \sigma:K_\bullet(X_t)\xrightarrow{\sim}K_\bullet(X_{t'})\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_{s'}).\]

One thus finds a specialization homomorphism on \(K_\bullet(X_t)\), but with values not in \(K_\bullet(X_s)\), but in \(K_\bullet(X_{s'})\), where \(X_{s'}\) is deduced from \(X_s\) by an extension \(k(s)\longrightarrow k(s')\) of the base fields (which can be assumed to be an extension of finite type if \(S\) is universally Japanese). Passing to geometric fibers as explained in 7.16, one similarly finds a homomorphism
\[(7.17.2)\qquad \sigma:K_\bullet(X_{\bar t})\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_{\bar s'});\]

so here again, the "geometric" specialization homomorphism takes its values not in \(K_\bullet(X_{\bar s})\), but in \(K_\bullet(X_{\bar s'})\), where \(X_{\bar s'}\) is deduced from \(X_{\bar s}\) by the extension of algebraically closed fields \(k(\bar s)\longrightarrow k(\bar s')\).

7.17.3. In general, the homomorphism \(K_\bullet(X_{\bar s})\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_{\bar s'})\) is not an isomorphism (because of the possible presence of "continuous" components in the group \(K_\bullet(X_{\bar s})\)). However, it is easy to check that it is an isomorphism modulo algebraic equivalence (7.11), and also modulo numerical equivalence (4.5.1) if \(f\) is smooth and proper. Consequently, using 7.12, one finds that (7.17.2) induces a specialization homomorphism
\[(7.17.3.1)\qquad K_\bullet^{\mathrm{alg}}(X_{\bar t})\longrightarrow K_\bullet^{\mathrm{alg}}(X_{\bar s});\]
and if \(f\) is proper and smooth, and provided that the specialization homomorphism (7.17.2) is compatible with numerical equivalence (cf. 7.9.4), one similarly finds a homomorphism
\[(7.17.3.2)\qquad K_\bullet^{\mathrm{num}}(X_{\bar t})\longrightarrow K_\bullet^{\mathrm{num}}(X_{\bar s}).\]

We leave to the reader the task of developing (if need be) the corresponding constructions for \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet\), \(K'\), and \(\operatorname{Gr}'\), and the properties of these specialization homomorphisms which follow trivially from the properties developed in the preceding sections. Let us only point out

that it follows from 7.16.2 that \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet^{\mathrm{num}}(X_{\bar t})\) is isomorphic to a quotient of a subgroup of \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet^{\mathrm{num}}(X_{\bar s})\) (\(f\) proper and smooth); in particular, its rank is again bounded by the rank of the latter.

7.17.4. When \(S\) is regular at \(s\) and \(t\) is maximal, one can construct \(S'\) by blowing up \(s\) in the localization of \(S\) at \(s\), and localizing at the generic point of the fiber of \(s\) in the blow-up. One then finds for \(k(s')\) a pure extension of \(k(s)\). Now it is not difficult to show that if \(k'\) is a pure extension of a field \(k\), then for every noetherian scheme \(T\) over \(k\), the canonical homomorphism \(K_\bullet(T)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(T\otimes_k k')\) is an isomorphism, by representing \(k'\) as an inductive limit of the affine rings of affine open sets of schemes of the form \(\operatorname{Spec} k[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\), and using (IX 1.6). Hence one concludes from this that (7.17.1) can be regarded as a canonical homomorphism
\[(7.17.4.1)\qquad K_\bullet(X_t)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s).\]

But it will be noted that one cannot deduce from it a homomorphism \(K_\bullet(X_{\bar t})\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_{\bar s})\), for on passing to a finite extension \(K_i\) of \(K=k(t)\), and normalizing \(S\) in \(K_i\), the regularity hypothesis on \(S\) will be lost.

Another way of defining a homomorphism (7.17.4.1) consists in reducing to the case where \(S\) is local with closed point \(s\) (which is trivial), and considering an increasing sequence of regular closed subschemes \(S_i\) of \(S\), with generic point \(t_i\), such that \(\dim S_i=i\) (\(0\leq i\leq n=\dim S\)). Since for every \(i\), \(0\leq i\leq n-1\), \(\mathcal O_{S_{i+1},t_i}\) is a discrete valuation ring with residue field \(k(t_i)\) and field of fractions \(k(t_{i+1})\), one has a specialization homomorphism
\[K_\bullet(X_{t_{i+1}})\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_{t_i}),\]

and the composite of these homomorphisms for \(0\leq i\leq n-1\) defines a homomorphism (7.17.4.1), which one can show (proceeding in a way analogous to 7.3) to be the same as the one defined previously by means of a blow-up. The advantage of the second method is however that here one even has homomorphisms of the form (7.16.1):
\[K_\bullet(X_{\bar t_{i+1}})\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_{\bar t_i}),\]
(with suitable coherent choices of the algebraic closures \(k(\bar t_i)\) of the \(k(t_i)\)), which allow one to define by composition a homomorphism
\[(7.17.4.2)\qquad K_\bullet(X_{\bar t})\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_{\bar s}),\]
which no doubt depends in an essential way on the choice of the \(S_i\), that is to say of the \(t_i\).

7.17.5. One can generalize the construction of a specialization homomorphism (7.17.4.2) to the case where one no longer assumes that \(S\) is regular at \(s\) and \(t\) a maximal point, provided one has resolution of singularities for the integral closed subscheme \(T\) of \(S\) with generic point \(t\), or simply for its localization at \(s\); this hypothesis is satisfied in particular if \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\) is excellent of characteristic zero, thanks to Hironaka. Indeed, one can then find a morphism \(S'\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{T,s})\), with \(S'\) regular, and taking a closed point of the fiber \(S'_s\), one can apply the above to \(X'=X\times_S S'\) over \(S'\), the generic point \(t'\) of \(S'\) and its closed point \(s'\). Whence a homomorphism of the form (7.17.4.2), since \(k(s')\) is a finite extension of \(k(s)\) and therefore has the same algebraic closure.
7.17.6. In all the constructions contemplated in section 7.17, we reduced, for the construction of a specialization homomorphism, to the

case where the base is a trait. It follows that the properties of the specialization homomorphism reviewed in the preceding sections, in the case where the base is a trait, extend automatically to the case of a more general base. Of course, the definition procedures contemplated apply just as well to \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet\), \(K'\) (in the case where \(f\) is smooth) and \(\operatorname{Gr}'\) (when moreover \(f\) is separated).

7.17.7. To finish, let us point out that the definition of a homomorphism (7.17.3.1) or (7.17.3.2), or that of a homomorphism (7.17.4.2), depends in an essential way on certain arbitrary choices (a suitable trait above \(S\) in 7.17.3, a sequence of \((S_i)\) in 7.17.4, and in addition the datum of a resolution of singularities in 7.17.5), and similarly for the variants for \(\operatorname{Gr}\), etc. One constructs obvious examples, with \(s\) an ordinary double point of an algebraic curve say, and \(X\) a projective and smooth scheme over \(S\) (for example an abelian scheme of relative dimension 2), where the specialization homomorphism on \(\operatorname{Gr}^1=\operatorname{Pic}\) and even on \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{alg}}^1=\operatorname{NS}\) (Néron-Severi group) actually depends on the choice of the trait \(S'\) chosen above \(S\), or what amounts to the same thing here, on the choice of a point above \(s\) of the normalization of \(S\).
7.18. Appendix to the Appendix: extension of the base field for certain functors in commutative groups
We make explicit here a technical point raised in 7.17.4.

7.18.1. Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) the category of integral \(k\)-algebras and flat homomorphisms of such algebras, \(F\) a functor on \(C\), with values in the category (Ens) of sets. Suppose that \(F\) commutes with filtered inductive limits. Let \(K\) be an extension of \(k\); we seek cri-
teria for \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(K)\) to be injective (resp. surjective).
7.18.2. Suppose that we are in one of the following two cases:
a) The field \(k\) is separably closed, and \(K/k\) is a separable extension.
b) The field \(k\) is infinite, and \(K\) is a pure extension of it.
Then the map \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(K)\) is injective.

Indeed, using the fact that \(K\) is a filtered inductive limit of subalgebras of finite type over \(k\), with flat transition morphisms, and the commutation of \(F\) with filtered inductive limits, we are reduced in case a) to proving that if \(A\) is an integral algebra of finite type over \(k\), whose field of fractions \(K\) is separable over \(k\), then \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(A)\) is injective. Now the hypotheses made on \(k\) and on \(K\) imply that there exists a \(k\)-homomorphism \(A\longrightarrow k\); then the composite \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(A)\longrightarrow F(k)\) is the identity, whence the conclusion. In case b), we reduce similarly, by passage to the limit, to proving that if \(A\) is an integral algebra of finite type over \(k\) such that its field of fractions is a pure extension of \(k\), then \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(A)\) is injective. Since \(k\) is infinite, the hypothesis again implies that there exists a \(k\)-homomorphism \(A\longrightarrow k\), and we finish as above.
7.18.3. Suppose now that \(F\) is a functor with values in the category (Ab) of abelian groups, and suppose that for every finite extension \(k'\) of \(k\), a norm homomorphism has been defined
\[N_{k'/k}:F(k')\longrightarrow F(k)\]
such that the composite
\[F(k)\longrightarrow F(k')\xrightarrow{N_{k'/k}}F(k)\]
is multiplication by \([k':k]\). Under these conditions, we have:
(i) For every extension \(K\) of \(k\), the kernel of \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(K)\) is a torsion group.
(ii) For every pure extension \(K\) of \(k\), the homomorphism \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(K)\) is injective. (NB. Note that we do not necessarily assume \(k\) infinite).

In case (i), we are reduced to proving that every element \(x\) in the kernel of \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(A)\), where \(A\) is an integral algebra of finite type over \(k\), is a torsion element. Using a \(k\)-homomorphism \(A\longrightarrow k'\), where \(k'\) is a finite \(k\)-algebra, we are reduced to the case where \(A=k'\) is a finite extension of \(k\). Then we have \([k':k]x=0\) by virtue of the property postulated above for the norm homomorphism. This proves that \(x\) is a torsion element.

We will even have \(x=0\) in the case where the gcd of the degrees of the finite extensions \(k'\) of \(k\) which are quotients of \(A\) is equal to 1. Now this is the case if the field of fractions \(K\) of \(A\) is a pure extension of \(k\), as one easily sees (only the case where \(k\) is finite requiring a new verification). This remark then gives the conclusion of (ii).

7.18.4. Suppose that for every non-empty affine open \(U\) of the affine space \(\operatorname{Spec} k[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\), if \(A\) is the ring of \(U\), the homomorphism \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(A)\) is surjective (compare IX 1.6). Then, using the commutation of \(F\) with filtered inductive limits, one finds that for every pure extension \(K\) of \(k\), \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(K)\) is surjective. If one assumes moreover \(k\) infinite, or that one can define norm homomorphisms as in 7.18.3, one concludes that this homomorphism is even bijective.
7.18.5. By way of example, consider a scheme \(X\) of finite type over \(k\), and set
\[F(A)=K_\bullet(X\otimes_k A),\]

which is a functor on the category \(C\) \(grâce à (IV 2.12)\). By virtue of (IV 3.2.3), the latter commutes with filtered inductive limits. Moreover, if \(k'\) is a finite extension of \(k\), then the projection \(p:X\otimes_k k'\longrightarrow X\) is a finite, flat morphism of finite presentation, hence it defines a direct image homomorphism \(p=N_{k'/k}:K_\bullet(X\otimes_k k')\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\). One verifies immediately that the condition contemplated in 7.18.3 on the norm homomorphisms is indeed satisfied. Finally, if \(A\) is the affine algebra of an open \(U\) of the affine space \(\operatorname{Spec} k[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\), then \(F(k)\longrightarrow F(A)\) is identified with the map \(K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X\times_k U)\), which is surjective for \(X\) noetherian, by virtue of IX 1.6. Then using the results proved above, one finds:
(i) If \(K/k\) is a pure extension of \(k\), then \(K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_K)\) is injective, and even bijective if \(X\) is noetherian.
(ii) If \(k\) is separably closed and \(K/k\) a separable extension, then \(K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_K)\) is injective.
(iii) For every extension \(K\) of \(k\), the kernel of \(K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_K)\) is a torsion group.

These results remain valid if one only assumes \(X\) noetherian, provided one restricts to extensions \(K\) of \(k\) which are of finite type (so that \(X_K\) is again of finite type). Moreover, one obtains essentially the same results for \(K'\) instead of the functor \(K_\bullet\), with the sole difference that in (i), one cannot in general assert bijectivity, for lack of the homotopy theorem for \(K'\) instead of \(K_\bullet\); to be able to conclude bijectivity, one would have to assume \(X\) noetherian regular, which implies that the groups \(K'\) and \(K_\bullet\) are the same for \(X\), resp. for \(X_K\).

One again obtains essentially the same results for \(\operatorname{Gr}'\) instead of \(K_\bullet\), or \(K'\), with the same reservation concerning the bijectivity assertion in (i). In the case where one restricts to \(\operatorname{Gr}^1=\operatorname{Pic}\), this bijectivity assertion is valid if \(X\) is noetherian and normal, for in this case, for every open \(U\) of an affine space over \(k\), one sees at once that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}(X\times_k U)\) is surjective (EGA ErrIV 53, 21.4.13).
